### 4.6 Cutover playbook (atomic-ish)

Phase order:
1. **(off-hours window)** Confirm no players online: `curl https://ss14.zig.computer/status` shows `players: 0`.
2. **On pico**: build wrapper binary, install to `/Users/pico/bin/ss14-wrapper`. Run sanity: `ss14-wrapper --healthcheck` returns 0.
3. **On pico**: install + load `com.zig.ss14-wrapper.plist`. Verify with `launchctl list | grep ss14-wrapper` (status `0` = running clean).
4. **On pico**: patch `config.toml` to `port = 1213` + bindto `127.0.0.1` + connectaddress `udp://ss14.zig.computer:1212` + `[net.wrapper]` block.
5. **On pico**: load `com.zig.ss14-watchdog.plist`. Verify Robust.Server bound `127.0.0.1:1213`: `lsof -i :1213 -P | grep LISTEN`.
6. **On zig-computer**: write `/etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-game.conf` per §4.1. `sudo nginx -t`.
7. **On zig-computer**: stop existing ss14-watchdog: `sudo systemctl stop ss14-watchdog`. Verify `:1212` free: `ss -tunap | grep :1212` shows nothing.
8. **On zig-computer**: `sudo systemctl reload nginx`. Verify nginx binds `:1212`: `ss -tunap | grep :1212` shows nginx workers.
9. **Verification**: synthetic test from a third tailnet device — `nc -u zig-computer-public-IP 1212 < known-bytes`. tcpdump on pico's wrapper-side confirms PROXY header arrives intact (PROXY v1 text on UDP per /check dotfiles-9cj; PROXY v2 binary on TCP). `nc -U /Users/pico/run/ss14-wrapper.sock` + `ENUMERATE\n` shows the session.
10. **Real-player verification**: connect with SS14 launcher. Issue a test `ban_address` from admin tools. `psql -c "SELECT address FROM ban_address ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 1"` returns the **real** client IP, not zig-computer's tailnet IP. PASS.
11. **On zig-computer**: `sudo systemctl disable ss14-watchdog`. Move `ss14-replay-rotate.timer` off zig-computer; load `com.zig.ss14-replay-rotate.plist` on pico.

Rollback: stop wrapper + watchdog on pico; on zig-computer remove `streams-enabled/ss14-game.conf`, `nginx -s reload`, re-enable+start `ss14-watchdog.service`. Pre-cutover state fully restored.

### 4.7 New cvar — `net.wrapper_enabled`

In `RobustToolbox/Robust.Shared/CVars.cs` (or content-layer equivalent if we want to keep Robust pristine — see §6 OQ-04):

```csharp
public static readonly CVarDef<bool> NetWrapperEnabled =
    CVarDef.Create("net.wrapper_enabled", false, CVar.ARCHIVE | CVar.SERVERONLY);

public static readonly CVarDef<string> NetWrapperSocket =
    CVarDef.Create("net.wrapper_socket", "/var/run/ss14-wrapper.sock", CVar.ARCHIVE | CVar.SERVERONLY);
```

## 5. Test Cases

### TC01 — PROXY header parse (wrapper unit) — v1 text format AND v2 binary autodetect
INPUT (a): hand-crafted UDP datagram with PROXY v1 text header `PROXY TCP4 203.0.113.5 100.95.4.73 54321 1212\r\n` + 16-byte payload `"ss14-handshake"` (this is the format nginx 1.28 actually emits on UDP, per /check dotfiles-9cj).
INPUT (b): hand-crafted UDP datagram with PROXY v2 binary header (12-byte signature `\x0D\x0A\x0D\x0A\x00\x0D\x0A\x51\x55\x49\x54\x0A` + version/family/length + AF_INET addrs) src `203.0.113.5:54321` + dst `100.95.4.73:1212` + 16-byte payload `"ss14-handshake"`.
EXPECTED (both): the autodetecting `proxyproto.Read(bufio.NewReader(...))` reader returns a `Header` whose `SourceAddr` resolves to `203.0.113.5:54321`, the payload offset equals the header length (47 bytes for v1 input above, 28 bytes for the v2 input), and the stripped payload bytes equal the input bytes after the offset.
RATIONALE: confirms the wrapper correctly peels both header formats (nginx 1.28 sends v1, but the library is autodetecting so a future nginx version switching to v2 still works without code changes).

### TC02 — Wrapper session map: NEW session creates outbound socket
INPUT: First datagram from client `203.0.113.5:54321` arrives at wrapper's listener.
EXPECTED: `SessionStore.LookupOrCreate` returns `isNew=true`, allocates a unique loopback source port, records the mapping (`fan-out-port → 203.0.113.5:54321`).
RATIONALE: confirms unique fan-out per real client, so Robust.Server sees distinct peers.

### TC03 — Wrapper session map: SECOND datagram from same 5-tuple reuses session, header re-parsed
INPUT: After TC02, a second datagram from `203.0.113.5:54321` arrives — and ALSO carries a PROXY v1 text header (per /check dotfiles-9cj, nginx 1.28 emits the header on EVERY UDP datagram, not just the first).
EXPECTED: Wrapper re-parses the PROXY header, extracts the same real-client IP `203.0.113.5:54321`, `LookupOrCreate` returns `isNew=false` (same srcAddr as TC02), fan-out port matches TC02's. Stripped payload is forwarded via the same loopback socket.
RATIONALE: confirms session-continuity under the actual nginx UDP behavior (header is per-datagram; the wrapper must tolerate re-seeing it without allocating a new fan-out port). Lidgren multiplexes packets; reordering must not lose state.

### TC04 — Wrapper UDS LOOKUP returns the real IP
INPUT: After TC02, a UDS client sends `LOOKUP udp <fan-out-port>\n`.
EXPECTED: Response `OK 203.0.113.5:54321\n`.
RATIONALE: confirms the API surface Robust.Server's patch will consume.

### TC05 — Wrapper UDS LOOKUP returns MISS for unknown port
INPUT: UDS client sends `LOOKUP udp 9999\n` where no session exists.
EXPECTED: Response `MISS\n`.
RATIONALE: confirms negative-path behavior; ensures Robust.Server falls back to raw endpoint.

### TC06 — End-to-end real-client IP appears in ban_address
INPUT: With full stack running (nginx + wrapper + Robust.Server), an SS14 launcher dials `ss14.zig.computer:1212` from client public IP `198.51.100.99`. Admin issues `/ban 198.51.100.99 testing`.
EXPECTED: `psql -c "SELECT address FROM ban_address ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 1"` returns `198.51.100.99`.
RATIONALE: the headline acceptance test; the entire spec exists to make this row correct.

### TC07 — Wrapper crash mid-game: Robust.Server stays up, moderation degrades to pass-through
INPUT: Send `kill -9` to the wrapper PID while a player is connected and a fresh connection arrives.
EXPECTED: (a) Robust.Server does not crash. (b) Already-connected player's session continues until next packet — then disconnects when wrapper-routed UDP stops arriving (acceptable; nginx will resume forwarding on wrapper restart). (c) During the gap, any `BanManager.BanMatchesPlayer` call's UDS lookup throws → patch returns `null` → fallback `raw` endpoint (`127.0.0.1:NNNN`) used. (d) Wrapper restarts within ~1 sec via LaunchAgent KeepAlive. (e) Post-restart, NEW connections re-establish with PROXY headers; OLD connections cycle.
RATIONALE: confirms graceful degradation per §3.9.

### TC08 — Wrapper UDP session TTL eviction
INPUT: Open a session via TC02, then wait `proxy_timeout` (120s per §4.1, set per OQ-01 decision) without any traffic.
EXPECTED: Wrapper's session store evicts the entry. A subsequent LOOKUP returns MISS. A new datagram from the same client triggers a fresh PROXY header from nginx + a fresh session in the wrapper.
RATIONALE: confirms session GC matches nginx's UDP session lifetime. Note: a real connected SS14 player generates ~1Hz Lidgren keepalive traffic (Robust overrides Lidgren `PingInterval = 1f` at NetManager.cs:655), so 120s eviction will only fire for genuinely idle sessions (paused-tab / NAT-rebind / lost-connection edge cases).

### TC09 — Concurrent sessions: 10 distinct clients
INPUT: Synthetically replay 10 distinct (src-IP, src-port) flows through the wrapper simultaneously.
EXPECTED: 10 unique fan-out ports allocated; 10 unique entries in `ENUMERATE`; Robust.Server's `RemoteEndPoint` per channel returns 10 distinct real IPs via LOOKUP.
RATIONALE: confirms the multiplexing model handles realistic player counts.

### TC10 — Bypass cvar default: with `net.wrapper_enabled=false`, no UDS calls happen
INPUT: Run Robust.Server with `net.wrapper_enabled=false` (default). Send a datagram.
EXPECTED: `NetChannel.RemoteEndPoint` returns `_connection.RemoteEndPoint` directly; no UDS connect attempted (verified via `strace`/`lsof` on the wrapper socket showing zero connections).
RATIONALE: confirms safe-default — turning off the patch returns to vanilla behavior.

### TC11 — TCP /status endpoint reachable + correct response
INPUT: From a client public IP, `curl http://ss14.zig.computer:1212/status` (TCP).
EXPECTED: HTTP 200, valid JSON `players:` field. With players connected, `players` count reflects them (gotcha #22's `admins_count_in_playercount=true` already in config).
RATIONALE: confirms TCP path through the stream block works; admin tooling that polls `/status` is unaffected.

### TC12 — IPv4-only first ship: connecting client over IPv6 is gracefully refused
INPUT: SS14 launcher dials `ss14.zig.computer:1212` over IPv6 (forced via launcher config).
EXPECTED: Connection fails cleanly OR succeeds via the nginx server listening on IPv4 only — with a clear server log noting "no IPv6 listener." NOT a silent hang or RemoteEndPoint = `::1`.
RATIONALE: IPv6 is out of scope first ship; behavior must be predictable (see §6 OQ-08 for future work).

### TC13 — nginx hot-reload preserves in-flight UDP sessions
INPUT: With wrapper + watchdog running and a real player connected, `sudo systemctl reload nginx` on zig-computer.
EXPECTED: Player's session continues without disconnect (PROXY-header sessions are re-emitted by nginx on next packet of the existing 5-tuple; wrapper either continues using the existing session OR re-creates from the new header — both are OK as long as the player doesn't drop).
RATIONALE: critical for ops; if hot-reload kicks every player, we can't iterate the nginx config.

### TC14 — GeoIP / IPIntel cache reads real IP
INPUT: With `net.wrapper_enabled=true` AND `game.ipintel_enabled=true`, a fresh connection from a known IP `203.0.113.5`. Pre-seed `ipintel_cache` table with `(203.0.113.5, score=0)`.
EXPECTED: `IPIntel.IsVpnOrProxy` query hits cache for `203.0.113.5` (NOT for the wrapper's loopback `127.0.0.1:NNNN`). `_db.GetIPIntelCache(ip)` call's `ip` parameter is `203.0.113.5`.
RATIONALE: confirms the patch reaches the IPIntel code path correctly — this is the #1 path the original Phase 4 rollback called out as broken.

### TC15 — `AdminAlertIfSharedConnection` correctly identifies real shared IPs
INPUT: Two distinct SS14 clients connect from the SAME real public IP `198.51.100.50` (e.g., siblings sharing home NAT). `CCVars.AdminAlertMinPlayersSharingConnection = 2`.
EXPECTED: Admin alert fires with the message naming both player usernames (because both `session.Channel.RemoteEndPoint.Address` reads via the patch return `198.51.100.50`).
RATIONALE: confirms the shared-connection alert isn't broken by the proxy; one of the moderation paths the parent bead specifically named.

### TC16 — Concurrent connect-storm of 30 players
INPUT: 30 synthetic SS14-handshake-like UDP flows arrive within a 5-second window.
EXPECTED: Wrapper allocates 30 fan-out ports without contention; UDS LOOKUP latency p99 < 5ms; no UDP datagrams dropped; Robust.Server's auth pipeline completes for all 30 inside the normal handshake budget.
RATIONALE: load test; confirms the wrapper isn't a bottleneck at typical SS14 server capacity (`net.max_connections = 256` default).

### TC17 — Cutover playbook is fully reversible
INPUT: Execute §4.6 steps 1-11 forward, then execute the rollback flow.
EXPECTED: Post-rollback, `ss -tunap | grep :1212` on zig-computer shows Robust.Server (not nginx) bound; pico's launchctl shows wrapper + watchdog unloaded; nginx config has no stream block for `:1212`; a real client connects successfully to the pre-cutover state.
RATIONALE: ops safety — must be able to revert in one shell session if anything breaks.

### TC18 — PROXY header malformed: wrapper logs + drops, doesn't crash
INPUT: Send a datagram to the wrapper's public listener with the first bytes set to garbage — neither a valid v1 text prefix (`PROXY ` ASCII) nor the v2 binary signature (`\x0D\x0A\x0D\x0A\x00\x0D\x0A\x51\x55\x49\x54\x0A`). The `proxyproto.Read` autodetecting parser must return an error.
EXPECTED: Wrapper increments a "malformed_header" metric, logs at WARN level, drops the packet. Wrapper process stays up. (Both v1 and v2 parse errors handled symmetrically — autodetecting library raises `proxyproto.ErrNoProxyProtocol` or similar; wrapper treats any non-nil error as drop-with-metric.)
RATIONALE: hardening — a malformed header arriving (whether from nginx misconfig or from a probe) must not be a crash vector, and the test must exercise the autodetect failure path for both PROXY format versions.

## 6. Open Questions

Surfaced via a self-interrogator pass after drafting Sections 1-5; severity-ranked P1 (blocks impl) / P2 (impl can proceed with risk) / P3 (nice-to-resolve).

### OQ-01 (P1) — How is the wrapper's UDP "session" key reconciled with Lidgren's NetConnection lifetime? **DECIDED 2026-05-24 via /check (dotfiles-9cj)**
nginx's UDP-session model evicts after `proxy_timeout` (nginx UDP default is 600s, NOT 60s — the spec draft's 60s was a test-deployment value, corrected here). Lidgren's NetConnection has its own lifetime (driven by keepalive + disconnect handshake). If a Lidgren session is still "alive" but nginx evicted the UDP session (e.g., on a paused tab), the next datagram from the client gets a NEW fan-out port at the wrapper — Robust.Server sees it as a NEW peer and starts a fresh handshake, mid-game. Behavior: the client gets disconnected and must reconnect.
- **Resolution**: set nginx `proxy_timeout 120s` (applied in §4.1). Grounded in source: Robust overrides Lidgren `PingInterval = 1f` at `RobustToolbox/Robust.Shared/Network/NetManager.cs:655`, so any connected player generates UDP traffic every ~1s, comfortably under the 120s window with ~2-minute headroom for transient packet loss / NAT rebinding. See `--notes` block on this bead for the full Lidgren-source citation chain.
- **Recommendation (historical, superseded)**: tune `proxy_timeout` to `300s` or higher. Decided down to 120s as the tighter production setting.
- **Affects**: §4.1 nginx config (applied), §4.2 wrapper session store (no change needed), runbook gotcha entry (deferred to zig-zone runbook update bead).

### OQ-02 (P1) — Session lifetime + cleanup semantics on the Robust.Server side **DECIDED 2026-05-24 via /check (dotfiles-9cj)**
When wrapper restarts (TC07), Robust.Server holds NetChannels that point to now-stale 127.0.0.1:NNNN fan-out ports. Does Robust.Server (or Lidgren) correctly detect "peer is gone" within a reasonable timeout (i.e., Lidgren's existing dead-connection detection), or do we leak channels indefinitely?
- **Resolution**: ACCEPT spec §3.9 — rely on Lidgren's existing `ConnectionTimeout` (default 25s; verified at `RobustToolbox/Lidgren.Network/Lidgren.Network/Lidgren.Network/NetPeerConfiguration.cs:123-124`; not overridden server-side). Wrapper is **stateless across restarts** — no session-map persistence to disk; each wrapper process starts empty and re-populates lazily from incoming per-datagram PROXY headers (matches §3.8 as updated). Wrapper restart costs every connected player a ~25s reconnect window via Lidgren's natural dead-peer timeout. Counter-design (wrapper-supplied "session-gone" UDS signal) rejected — more patch surface for marginal improvement; if 25s proves unacceptable post-shipping, add as follow-up. See `--notes` block on this bead for the full reasoning.
- **Recommendation (historical, ratified)**: rely on Lidgren's existing `ConnectionTimeout` — this is now the chosen design.
- **Affects**: §3.9 failure semantics (no change — ratified); §3.8 (added stateless-wrapper note); §4.3 patch surface (no change — no new UDS message types).

### OQ-03 (P1) — Does nginx 1.28 actually emit PROXY v2 on UDP, or only TCP? **DECIDED 2026-05-24 via /check (dotfiles-9cj)**
The nginx changelog confirms `proxy_protocol on` for UDP landed in 1.27.x but the Ubuntu questing-shipped 1.28.0 build's behavior should be verified BEFORE committing. nginx -V shows `--with-stream=dynamic --with-stream_realip_module`, but realip is for INBOUND PROXY (the opposite direction).
- **Resolution**: nginx 1.28 DOES emit PROXY-protocol on UDP — but it's **v1 (text format)**, not v2 (binary). Verified empirically via a synthetic stream config on test port `:11212` forwarding to a Python UDP sniffer at `127.0.0.1:11213` (live SS14 on `:1212` untouched, test config cleanly rolled back). Every datagram arrived with a `PROXY TCP4 <real-src-ip> <dst-ip> <src-port> <dst-port>\r\n` header prepended (NOT the v2 binary signature). Two derived corrections applied to the spec: (a) the wrapper uses `proxyproto.Read(bufio.NewReader(...))` autodetecting reader (handles v1 and v2 transparently) instead of the v2-only `ParseV2`; (b) the header is emitted on EVERY UDP datagram (per-datagram), not "once per UDP session" as the spec draft assumed — see §3.8 and §4.2 updates. Design holds; the C2 architecture is fully validated. See `--notes` block on this bead for the full test method, evidence bytes, and rollback verification.
- **Recommendation (historical, superseded)**: pre-impl synthetic test before committing. Done; corrections applied.
- **Affects**: §3.1 (clarified v1-text), §3.8 (per-datagram header note), §4.1 (proxy_timeout 120s — orthogonal but co-decided), §4.2 (autodetecting reader), TC01 + TC03 + TC18 (v1+v2 autodetect coverage).

### OQ-04 (P2) — Should the IRemoteAddressOverride hook live in RobustToolbox or in Content.Server?
Patching `Robust.Shared/Network/NetManager.NetChannel.cs` introduces a content-layer concern (the IRemoteAddressOverride interface dep) into the engine. Alternatives: (a) Define the interface in Robust.Shared as a totally optional hook (default null = pass-through); (b) Use C#'s `event` mechanism so Content.Server subscribes without changing the engine signature; (c) Subclass NetChannel — but it's `sealed`.
- **Recommendation**: (a) Interface in Robust.Shared, content-layer implementation. Smallest engine diff, minimal coupling. Means a tiny patch to Robust.Shared per impl, but acceptable for a forked engine.
- **Affects**: §3.10 (fork-maintenance burden), §4.3 patch shape.

### OQ-05 (P2) — Wrapper language: confirm Go choice vs Rust
Decision §3.6 picked Go. P2 because the choice is reversible up to the impl-bead start. Risk vector: if a future maintainer is more comfortable in Rust, Go choice adds bus factor.
- **Recommendation**: stay with Go. Rust's safety benefits don't materially matter for a small ~500 LOC UDP-forward + UDS-server; Go's ecosystem for PROXY-protocol parse (`github.com/pires/go-proxyproto`) is mature; cross-compile to darwin-arm64 is one-line.
- **Affects**: §3.6, §4.2 implementation language.

### OQ-06 (P2) — UDS vs HTTP for the wrapper API
Decision §3.7 picked UDS. Risk: UDS-on-macOS sometimes has filesystem-mode subtleties (e.g., parent dir permissions blocking the bind even when the socket file itself is mode-0660). HTTP-loopback is more universal at the cost of port allocation + slightly higher latency.
- **Recommendation**: stay with UDS. The Robust.Server process and the wrapper both run as `pico:staff`; the socket path `/Users/pico/run/` is owner-controlled; modes are predictable. Document the dir-creation step in the LaunchAgent.
- **Affects**: §4.2 UDS path config, §4.4 LaunchAgent (must `mkdir -p /Users/pico/run` before bind).

### OQ-07 (P2) — How does Robust.Server know which (proto, port) to LOOKUP for `NetConnectingArgs.IP` at AUTH time?
At the auth-time site (NetManager.ServerAuth.cs:231), `connection.RemoteEndPoint` is the SAME port the wrapper's fan-out used to forward — i.e., `127.0.0.1:<fan-out-port>`. The wrapper's UDS LOOKUP key is `(udp, fan-out-port)`. So LOOKUP works. But the patch site needs to KNOW the proto (`udp` vs `tcp`); for SS14 the handshake itself is over UDP, but the /status endpoint is TCP and there's a TCP "info" exchange during launcher handshake (pre-game).
- **Recommendation**: NetChannel inherently knows its proto via the underlying NetConnection's socket family. Pass `connection.Socket.ProtocolType` to the Lookup call; route UDS LOOKUP with the correct proto string. Add a fallback that tries `udp` then `tcp` if proto is ambiguous.
- **Affects**: §4.3 patch precision.

### OQ-08 (P2) — IPv6 dual-stack support: when (not if)?
Robust.Server's default `net.bindto = "0.0.0.0,::"` means dual-stack is the expected behavior. The spec ships IPv4 only first. Players who's only path to zig-computer is over IPv6 (rare but real) are excluded.
- **Recommendation**: ship IPv4 first (most production traffic). File a follow-up `task` bead to extend nginx stream + wrapper to handle IPv6: nginx PROXY v2 supports AF_INET6; wrapper needs minor changes to its session-key (use `netip.AddrPort` instead of `*net.UDPAddr` for cleaner v4/v6 handling).
- **Affects**: future work bead (Section 7).

### OQ-09 (P3) — Wrapper performance under sustained 30-Hz tickrate
At 30 ticks/sec × 30 players × ~700-byte packets = ~630 kbit/sec on the wrapper's UDP fan-out path. Trivial throughput; but the wrapper's PER-PACKET cost is one map read + one socket write. Go's `net.PacketConn` may allocate per `ReadFrom` if the buffer isn't reused; verify with `pprof`.
- **Recommendation**: use `sync.Pool` for the read buffer; profile under TC16; document the result in the impl bead.
- **Affects**: implementation detail.

### OQ-10 (P3) — vs14-mapserver coexistence on pico
The mapserver runs in a Colima container on pico (Phase 3). It binds Colima-bridged ports. The wrapper binds tailnet-IP:1212. No port overlap. But: Colima's port-forwarding sometimes flakes (gotcha #20). If wrapper's `:1212` doesn't appear externally because of Colima networking interaction, we'd see "tcpdump on zig-computer shows packets going out, but pico reports nothing arriving."
- **Recommendation**: wrapper binds the tailnet IP DIRECTLY (not through Colima). Verify with `lsof -i :1212 -P` showing the wrapper process, not a Colima `vpnkit` shim. Document the check in the cutover playbook.
- **Affects**: §4.6 cutover step 3 (the `launchctl list` verification).

### OQ-11 (P3) — Connection-establishment timing: PROXY-header round-trip vs SS14 handshake timeout
Lidgren's handshake has multiple round-trips (`MsgLoginStart` → `MsgEncryptionRequest` → `MsgEncryptionResponse` → `MsgLoginSuccess`). The wrapper's UDS LOOKUP happens DURING `NetMgrOnConnecting` event (which is in the middle of `NetManager.ServerAuth.cs`). The UDS round-trip is ~1ms; Lidgren's auth timeout is multiple seconds. Should be a non-issue.
- **Recommendation**: log UDS latency at the patch site; alert if p99 > 10ms; track in impl-bead. No action needed unless it materializes.
- **Affects**: monitoring detail.

### OQ-12 (P3) — Hot-reload of wrapper config without dropping sessions
If we ever need to change the wrapper's upstream port or socket path live, must we stop+start (losing sessions)? Or can we SIGHUP-reload?
- **Recommendation**: no SIGHUP support in v1. Restart loses sessions; players reconnect within Lidgren's timeout (~25s, per OQ-02). Document this as known op limitation.
- **Affects**: future feature.

### OQ-13 (P3) — Telemetry: where does the wrapper publish metrics?
Pico already has a Loki + Prometheus container (Phase 3). The wrapper could expose `/metrics` over HTTP for Prometheus to scrape.
- **Recommendation**: ship without metrics first; add Prometheus exporter as a follow-up bead. The text-protocol UDS already exposes `HEALTH` for spot-checks.
- **Affects**: future work.

## 7. Future Considerations

- **Upstream contribution**: If Space Wizards ever softens their "don't reverse-proxy UDP" stance, the IRemoteAddressOverride interface + the NetChannel hook are the minimal upstream-PR-able piece. The wrapper itself stays out-of-tree as a deployment artifact.
- **IPv6 support**: per OQ-08, file as separate task bead once IPv4 is shipped + stable for a week.
- **Performance scaling**: if SS14 ever pushes past ~256 concurrent connections (current default `net.max_connections`), revisit wrapper's per-session goroutine model — switch to a single epoll-style loop if profiling shows scheduler pressure.
- **Future netcode migration**: If SS14 ever moves off Lidgren (the upstream has occasionally discussed it), this entire wrapper approach becomes moot — the new netcode might natively support PROXY protocol or a similar mechanism. Track upstream RobustToolbox network refactor proposals.
- **Wrapper observability**: Prometheus `/metrics` endpoint exporting `sessions_active`, `lookups_per_sec`, `malformed_headers_total`, `uds_latency_seconds_bucket`. Wire into pico's existing Phase 3 observability stack.
- **DDOS protection at the wrapper layer**: currently inherited from nginx (which has rate-limit primitives) and the tailnet (which has ACL primitives). If real DDOS materializes, consider a `nginx limit_conn` block + wrapper-side connection-rate-limit.
- **Multi-fork compatibility**: if another SS14 community fork wants the same capability, the wrapper + the 2-file patch are extractable as a generic `RobustToolbox-net-wrapper` repo. No vacation-station-14 specifics in the wrapper code (it's just PROXY-protocol → UDS).
- **Re-link to mainline**: when Phase 4 lands per this spec, the zig-zone runbook's Phase 4 section gets rewritten from "ROLLED BACK" to "SHIPPED VIA C2". Two new gotchas likely: (a) nginx UDP PROXY emit version requirement, (b) wrapper-restart implies player-reconnect window. The earlier "definitive architectural argument" against reverse-proxied UDP becomes "definitive UNLESS wrapper-based source-preservation is in play."

## Decision: OQ-03 — DECIDED 2026-05-24 via /check (dotfiles-9cj)

**Question:** Does nginx 1.28 actually emit PROXY v2 on UDP, or only TCP?

**Verdict:** NEEDS-WORK — nginx 1.28 emits PROXY-protocol on UDP, but it's **v1 (text)** not **v2 (binary)**. Design holds; spec text needs updates.

**Test method:** Synthetic stream config at `/etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-proxyv2-test.conf` (test port :11212 — live SS14 on :1212 untouched). nginx forwarded to a Python UDP sniffer at 127.0.0.1:11213 that dumped raw bytes hex. Three test datagrams sent via `nc -u`:
- From 127.0.0.1 → 127.0.0.1:11212 (loopback)
- From 127.0.0.1 → 100.98.174.21:11212 (tailnet IP dst)
- From 100.98.174.21 → 127.0.0.1:11212 (tailnet IP src, explicit port via `nc -p 55555`)

**Evidence:** Every datagram arrived at sniffer with a textual PROXY v1 header prepended (NOT the v2 binary signature `\x0D\x0A\x0D\x0A\x00\x0D\x0A\x51\x55\x49\x54\x0A`). Example raw bytes:
```
PROXY TCP4 100.98.174.21 127.0.0.1 55555 11212\r\nOQ03-TEST-PAYLOAD-FROMTS
```
Header carries: real-client-IP `100.98.174.21`, real-dst-IP `127.0.0.1`, real-client-port `55555`, real-dst-port `11212`. Source-port pinning verified (explicit `-p 55555` → `55555` in header). The "TCP4" label is a known PROXY-v1 spec limitation (v1 only defines TCP4/TCP6; UDP-over-v1 reuses the TCP4 token per haproxy convention).

**Two assumption corrections vs spec §4.2:**
1. Spec assumed v2 binary header (`proxyproto.ParseV2`). Reality: v1 text. The Go library `github.com/pires/go-proxyproto` auto-detects v1 vs v2 via the first bytes — switching to its generic `Reader.ReadHeader` is a one-line wrapper change. No upstream re-architecture needed.
2. Spec assumed nginx emits header "ONCE per UDP session" (NetManager.NetChannel.cs:40 sketch in §4.2 reads "First packet: parse PROXY v2 header"). Reality: nginx 1.28 emits the header on **every UDP datagram** of a session, not just the first. This actually SIMPLIFIES the wrapper — no per-session header-state needed. The session-store still maps fan-out-port → real-client-IP for the Robust.Server LOOKUP API; the wrapper just parses the header on every UDP datagram and forwards the stripped payload.

**Rollback verified:** YES.
- Test config removed: `sudo rm /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-proxyv2-test.conf`
- nginx config diff vs pre-snapshot `/tmp/nginx-config-pre.1779650655.txt`: zero changes
- `:11212` no longer bound
- `:1212` still bound to Robust.Server (4 listeners: UDP+TCP × v4+v6) throughout — live SS14 untouched

**Spec impact (re-dispatch /spec to apply):**
- §3.1 — clarify "PROXY-protocol v1 text" instead of "v2 header"
- §4.1 nginx config — `proxy_protocol on` is correct; remove any v2-specific wording
- §4.2 wrapper pseudocode — replace `proxyproto.ParseV2(buf[:n])` with `proxyproto.Read(reader)` (autodetects v1/v2 via library), and remove the "First packet: parse PROXY v2 header" branching since header is per-datagram
- §4.2 SessionStore — no functional change, but the comment "nginx only emits header once per UDP session" is wrong and should be deleted
- TC01 — update to test v1 text-format parse (or both v1 and v2, exercising the library's autodetect)
- TC03 — update INPUT to "second datagram from same 5-tuple ALSO carries a PROXY header" and EXPECTED to "wrapper re-parses, gets same real-client IP, fan-out port unchanged"

## Decision: OQ-01 — DECIDED 2026-05-24 via /check (dotfiles-9cj)

**Question:** How is the wrapper's UDP "session" key reconciled with Lidgren's NetConnection lifetime? What `proxy_timeout` setting won't evict active players?

**Decision:** ACCEPT spec recommendation with refinement — set nginx `proxy_timeout 120s` (NOT the spec-draft's 60s, NOT OQ-01's 300s suggestion). Default nginx UDP `proxy_timeout` is actually **10 minutes** (600s) per nginx docs, NOT 60s as the spec text implied.

**Rationale (grounded in source):**
- Lidgren default `PingInterval = 4.0s`, `ConnectionTimeout = 25.0s` (verified at `RobustToolbox/Lidgren.Network/Lidgren.Network/Lidgren.Network/NetPeerConfiguration.cs:123-124`).
- Robust overrides `PingInterval = 1f` (1 second) in `RobustToolbox/Robust.Shared/Network/NetManager.cs:655` — comment: "ping the client once per second."
- Server-side `ConnectionTimeout` is NOT overridden in `NetManager._getBaseNetPeerConfig` (line 683-693 only sets it on the client branch). Server inherits Lidgren default 25s.
- `CVars.ConnectionTimeout` (`net.connection_timeout`, default 25.0s) is flagged `CVar.CLIENTONLY` per `RobustToolbox/Robust.Shared/CVars.cs:305`. So no server-side knob.
- Effective traffic floor for any connected player: 1Hz server→client pings + Lidgren acks. nginx UDP session sees traffic every ~1s minimum.
- Therefore: any `proxy_timeout >= 30s` is safe for active players. `proxy_timeout 120s` gives 2-minute headroom for transient packet loss (mobile clients on flaky wifi, NAT-rebinding pauses) without evicting a still-alive Lidgren peer.

**Refinement vs spec §4.1:** The spec's `proxy_timeout 60s` is OK but `120s` is better. Also: `proxy_responses 1` in §4.1 is CORRECT (Lidgren is request/response stateful, NOT fire-and-forget DNS-style; `proxy_responses 0` would break Lidgren's ack handshake — gotcha #23 already calls this out in the spec).

**Spec impact:**
- §4.1 nginx config — change `proxy_timeout 60s` to `proxy_timeout 120s` on the UDP block. Add a comment line citing Lidgren `PingInterval = 1f` from NetManager.cs:655 as the reason 120s is comfortably wide.
- OQ-01 description text — note that nginx default is 600s (not 60s); the spec's "60s" was the test-deployment value, not nginx's default.

## Decision: OQ-02 — DECIDED 2026-05-24 via /check (dotfiles-9cj)

**Question:** When wrapper restarts (TC07), Robust.Server holds NetChannels pointing to now-stale 127.0.0.1:NNNN fan-out ports. Does Lidgren correctly detect "peer is gone" within reasonable timeout, or do we leak channels?

**Decision:** ACCEPT spec §3.9 — rely on Lidgren's existing `ConnectionTimeout = 25s` for natural eviction. Wrapper is **stateless across restarts** (no session persistence). Document the ~25s reconnect window in the runbook as a known op cost of any wrapper restart.

**Rationale:**
- Server-side Lidgren detects dead peers when no inbound traffic arrives for `ConnectionTimeout = 25s` (verified above). With `PingInterval = 1s`, the server sends 25 pings before declaring death — each ping that fails to receive an ack contributes to the timeout countdown.
- Wrapper restart sequence: (a) wrapper crash → session-map lost; (b) LaunchAgent KeepAlive restarts wrapper within ~1s; (c) Robust.Server's outbound pings to old fan-out port go to wrapper, which has no mapping → wrapper drops them (no real-client target); (d) ~25s later, server marks peer dead, closes NetChannel; (e) any active player reconnects via launcher, gets a fresh PROXY-header packet → new session.
- Total worst-case player-side disruption: ~25s + reconnect latency. Acceptable for an unplanned restart; planned restarts can be off-hours.
- Counter-design ("session-gone" UDS signal from wrapper at startup, telling Robust.Server to drop all NetChannels) was considered and rejected:
  - More patch surface (new UDS message type, new server-side handler that walks NetChannels and closes them).
  - More interop coupling (wrapper has to enumerate "active sessions it knows about" and signal them; but the wrapper post-restart has NO knowledge of what was active pre-restart — the only honest signal is "I just started, my map is empty, please drop everything").
  - The 25s natural timeout already provides graceful degradation per spec §3.9.
- If post-shipping we find 25s is too long (e.g., LaunchAgent flapping causes player rage-quits), we can add the "session-gone" signal in a follow-up impl bead. v1 ships without it.

**Wrapper state-management decision: STATELESS.** No persistence of session-map to disk on shutdown. No restoration on startup. Each wrapper process starts with an empty map; populates lazily from incoming PROXY headers. Matches the spec's §3.9 graceful-degradation model.

**Spec impact:**
- §3.9 — no change; this decision RATIFIES the existing design.
- Runbook (zig-zone Phase 4 section, when migrated to SHIPPED via C2): add a gotcha entry "wrapper restart costs ~25s player reconnect window per Lidgren ConnectionTimeout default."
- TC07 EXPECTED text already aligns; no test-case change.

## Post-impl correction — §4.3 enumerates 2 patch sites, impl found 3 (2026-05-24)

Spec §4.3 listed 2 patch sites for IRemoteAddressOverride:
1. NetChannel construction (RemoteEndPoint getter) — patched in vs-4h1
2. NetManager.ServerAuth.cs:236 (NetConnectingArgs.IP synthesis) — patched in vs-4h1

Scrutiny on vs-4h1 (general-purpose subagent) found a **3rd patch site**
the spec missed:

3. **NetManager.ServerAuth.cs:49** (isLocal computation) — read RAW
   connection.RemoteEndPoint.Address to gate the "Auth Required" check.
   With wrapper in front, every client appears as 127.0.0.1 (wrapper
   forwards to loopback) → isLocal=true → "Auth Required" gate
   short-circuits → SILENT AUTH BYPASS on wrapper-deployed servers.
   Patched in vs-q7m (cherry-picked to vs14 main as 57b88cb987 +
   submodule branch ss14-fork/wrapper-remote-address-vs-4h1 at
   85982545e on azigler/RobustToolbox).

Lesson: §2.2's promise ("all moderation paths funnel through
INetChannel.RemoteEndPoint") is true for MODERATION READS but the
auth handshake has its own raw-address read for the loopback fast-path.
Any future spec work that adds wrapper-aware semantics should grep
the entire RobustToolbox codebase for raw RemoteEndPoint.Address reads,
not just consult §2.2.

Spec body NOT amended (would supersede the closed scrutiny artifact);
this note + the implementation commits are the canonical record.

- Server-side Lidgren detects dead peers when no inbound traffic arrives for `ConnectionTimeout = 25s` (verified above). With `PingInterval = 1s`, the server sends 25 pings before declaring death — each ping that fails to receive an ack contributes to the timeout countdown.
- Wrapper restart sequence: (a) wrapper crash → session-map lost; (b) LaunchAgent KeepAlive restarts wrapper within ~1s; (c) Robust.Server's outbound pings to old fan-out port go to wrapper, which has no mapping → wrapper drops them (no real-client target); (d) ~25s later, server marks peer dead, closes NetChannel; (e) any active player reconnects via launcher, gets a fresh PROXY-header packet → new session.
- Total worst-case player-side disruption: ~25s + reconnect latency. Acceptable for an unplanned restart; planned restarts can be off-hours.
- Counter-design ("session-gone" UDS signal from wrapper at startup, telling Robust.Server to drop all NetChannels) was considered and rejected:
  - More patch surface (new UDS message type, new server-side handler that walks NetChannels and closes them).
  - More interop coupling (wrapper has to enumerate "active sessions it knows about" and signal them; but the wrapper post-restart has NO knowledge of what was active pre-restart — the only honest signal is "I just started, my map is empty, please drop everything").
  - The 25s natural timeout already provides graceful degradation per spec §3.9.
- If post-shipping we find 25s is too long (e.g., LaunchAgent flapping causes player rage-quits), we can add the "session-gone" signal in a follow-up impl bead. v1 ships without it.

**Wrapper state-management decision: STATELESS.** No persistence of session-map to disk on shutdown. No restoration on startup. Each wrapper process starts with an empty map; populates lazily from incoming PROXY headers. Matches the spec's §3.9 graceful-degradation model.

**Spec impact:**
- §3.9 — no change; this decision RATIFIES the existing design.
- Runbook (zig-zone Phase 4 section, when migrated to SHIPPED via C2): add a gotcha entry "wrapper restart costs ~25s player reconnect window per Lidgren ConnectionTimeout default."
- TC07 EXPECTED text already aligns; no test-case change.

---

## Implementation Readiness — 2026-05-24

**P1 OQs decided:** 3/3 (OQ-01, OQ-02, OQ-03)
**P2 OQs decided:** 0/6 (deferred — not blocking impl)
**P3 OQs decided:** 0/4 (deferred — not blocking impl)
**Conflicts resolved:** N/A (none surfaced)
**Dependencies confirmed:** parent dotfiles-52c context absorbed; sibling dotfiles-r8k + dotfiles-jjd findings (TPROXY + GRE both rejected) re-confirmed as motivating C2

- Wrapper daemon (Go): READY (with PROXY-v1-text instead of v2-binary — library `go-proxyproto` autodetects; one-line change)
- Robust.Server patches: READY (no impact from any P1 decision — patches are protocol-format-agnostic)
- LaunchAgent + cutover playbook: READY

### Spec updates needed (re-dispatch /spec to apply before /test, OR roll into /test prompt as overrides)
1. **§3.1**: clarify "PROXY-protocol v1 text format" (not v2 binary)
2. **§4.1**: change `proxy_timeout 60s` → `proxy_timeout 120s` (rationale: Lidgren PingInterval = 1s)
3. **§4.2 wrapper pseudocode**: use `proxyproto.Read(reader)` autodetect (not `ParseV2`); remove "First packet only" branching; header is per-datagram
4. **§4.2 comment**: delete "nginx only emits header once per UDP session" — false per OQ-03 test
5. **TC01 + TC03**: update INPUT/EXPECTED to reflect per-datagram v1 text headers
6. **OQ-01 text**: note nginx UDP `proxy_timeout` default is 600s (not 60s as spec implied)

These are all editorial / parameter changes — no architectural impact. The C2 design is fully validated.

### Recommended impl order (per /orchestrator wave pattern)
1. Wave 1 — wrapper daemon (Go binary at `~/dotfiles/ss14/wrapper/`) + unit tests (TC01-TC05, TC08, TC09, TC16, TC18). No SS14 dependency. Can run on zig-computer dev box.
2. Wave 2 — Robust.Server patches (vacation-station-14 fork) + content-layer IRemoteAddressOverride implementation. Tests TC07 (with mock UDS), TC10 (cvar default off).
3. Wave 3 — LaunchAgent plists + dotfiles/vs14 template updates. Tests are config-validation only.
4. Wave 4 — Cutover playbook dry-run (off-hours, on pico in standalone mode). End-to-end TC06, TC11, TC13-TC15. Real-player verification TC17.

### Recommended next: /test dispatch
Test bead created from TC01-TC18 with the OQ-03 corrections folded in. Then /impl waves per above order.

## Post-impl correction — §4.3 enumerates 2 patch sites, impl found 3 (2026-05-24)

Spec §4.3 listed 2 patch sites for IRemoteAddressOverride:
1. NetChannel construction (RemoteEndPoint getter) — patched in vs-4h1
2. NetManager.ServerAuth.cs:236 (NetConnectingArgs.IP synthesis) — patched in vs-4h1

Scrutiny on vs-4h1 (general-purpose subagent) found a **3rd patch site**
the spec missed:

3. **NetManager.ServerAuth.cs:49** (isLocal computation) — read RAW
   connection.RemoteEndPoint.Address to gate the "Auth Required" check.
   With wrapper in front, every client appears as 127.0.0.1 (wrapper
   forwards to loopback) → isLocal=true → "Auth Required" gate
   short-circuits → SILENT AUTH BYPASS on wrapper-deployed servers.
   Patched in vs-q7m (cherry-picked to vs14 main as 57b88cb987 +
   submodule branch ss14-fork/wrapper-remote-address-vs-4h1 at
   85982545e on azigler/RobustToolbox).

Lesson: §2.2's promise ("all moderation paths funnel through
INetChannel.RemoteEndPoint") is true for MODERATION READS but the
auth handshake has its own raw-address read for the loopback fast-path.
Any future spec work that adds wrapper-aware semantics should grep
the entire RobustToolbox codebase for raw RemoteEndPoint.Address reads,
not just consult §2.2.

Spec body NOT amended (would supersede the closed scrutiny artifact);
this note + the implementation commits are the canonical record.

title: ssh: Host alias for pico — fixes plain ssh/scp/rsync over tailnet
description:
## Context

Plain `ssh pico` / `scp pico:` failed in mid-A1111-install with `Host key
verification failed.` Re-diagnosis revealed two stacked issues:

1. OpenSSH first-connect prompt has no TTY in non-interactive shells →
   `Host key verification failed.`
2. After accepting the key, OpenSSH defaults `User=$LOGNAME` (e.g. `ubuntu`
   on zig-computer); pico has no `ubuntu` user → tailscaled rejects with
   `failed to look up local user "ubuntu"`.

Originally documented as gotcha #17 in zig-zone runbook (incorrectly
framed as host-key-only). This bead captures the proper fix.

## Steps to Reproduce

On zig-computer (or any tailnet member that doesn't have a `Host pico`
alias and whose local username isn't `pico`):

1. `ssh pico` → `Host key verification failed.` (non-interactive shell
   has no TTY for the first-connect prompt)
2. `ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new pico` → first-connect passes,
   then `tailscale: failed to look up local user "ubuntu"` →
   `Connection closed by 100.x.y.z port 22` (pico has no ubuntu user)
3. Workaround that always works: `ssh pico@pico` — explicit user bypasses
   the second issue, accept-new bypasses the first

## Fix

Host alias in `~/.ssh/local` (gitignored, sourced by `Include local` in
committed `~/dotfiles/ssh/config`):

```
Host pico
    HostName pico.tailfb4637.ts.net
    User pico
    StrictHostKeyChecking accept-new
```

The committed `~/dotfiles/ssh/config` keeps no host-specific data — also
moved the pre-existing gpu block (OVH VPS IP) out to `~/.ssh/local` for
the same reason. The committed file now has just `Include local` plus a
convention comment pointing future-you at the pattern.

## Regression guard

- Convention comment in committed `~/dotfiles/ssh/config` is the structural
  guard — future agents reading the file see "per-host config lives in
  ~/.ssh/local."
- gotcha #17 in zig-zone runbook rewrite is the procedural guard — when
  the symptom resurfaces (fresh dotfiles install on a new machine that
  needs pico access), the gotcha tells you what to add and why.

## Acceptance criteria

- [x] Plain `ssh pico` works (no @user, no flags)
- [x] Plain `scp pico:` works
- [x] No tailnet FQDN / private IPs committed to dotfiles
- [x] gpu block moved out of committed dotfiles (Include-local pattern)
- [x] zig-zone runbook gotcha #17 rewritten with correct root cause
- [x] Comment in `~/dotfiles/ssh/config` documents the convention

title: fix: bench wrapper false-TIMEOUT verdict — kimi-linear correctly renamed but killed before DONE ack
description:
## Problem
Smoke matrix scenario 01-trivial-rename for `kimi-linear` was tagged `verdict_tag: TIMEOUT` (claude exit code 125, killed at 1200s wall cap). But the sandbox state at trial-end shows the file was renamed CORRECTLY:

    FILECONTENTS:/tmp/claude-local-test/s01/calc.py:def sum_two(a, b):\n    """Sum two numbers."""\n    return a + b\n\n\nif __name__ == "__main__":\n    print(sum_two(2, 3))

Both call sites match the spec. The model did the work — it just didn't print "DONE" before the wrapper killed it at the 20-min cap.

## Symptom
Reading the Tier-1 dashboard (or the scoreboard markdown), kimi-linear looks like a failure. It is actually a success-but-slow-ack case — qualitatively different from "task incomplete + timed out."

## Suspected cause
The bench wrapper (`run-scenario.sh` or similar) computes `verdict_tag` purely from claude's exit code, ignoring the post-run sandbox snapshot. A `FILECONTENTS` line that matches the spec's expected output (provided by the scenario file) is strong evidence of task completion, but the wrapper doesn't consult it.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run the smoke matrix on a model whose tokens-per-second is borderline at the wall_cap_seconds budget
2. Model successfully completes the edits but spends remaining time emitting "DONE"
3. Wrapper SIGKILLs at the cap; claude exits 125
4. verdict_tag = TIMEOUT despite the file showing the task done

## Fix sketch
Add a post-kill verification step to the wrapper: when verdict_tag is about to be TIMEOUT, check whether the sandbox state matches `expected_filecontents` from the scenario. If yes, downgrade to `TIMEOUT_SUCCESS` (task done, slow ack) or similar 3-state verdict (OK / TIMEOUT_SUCCESS / TIMEOUT_FAIL). The dashboard should distinguish these.

Alternative: bump the wall_cap for slow-but-correct models on scenarios they're known to handle.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Wrapper distinguishes TIMEOUT_SUCCESS (file correct) from TIMEOUT_FAIL (file wrong/missing)
- [ ] Kimi-Linear's 2026-05-28 result re-verdicts to TIMEOUT_SUCCESS
- [ ] Dashboard rendering reflects the 3-state verdict
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Wrapper distinguishes TIMEOUT_SUCCESS (file correct) from TIMEOUT_FAIL (file wrong/missing)
 - [ ] Kimi-Linear 2026-05-28 result re-verdicts to TIMEOUT_SUCCESS
 - [ ] Dashboard renders the 3-state verdict

title: fix: zig-watchdog HermesGateway re-blocks tasks based on stale lifetime crash counter (post-unblock)
description:
## Problem

After the M2/Documentation fix (dotfiles-hpi) shipped, t_360fa078 was unblocked
to test the Hermes --skills fix. The worker spawned, got past the "Unknown
skill(s)" error, and "Initializing agent..." fired — but ~3 minutes later the
zig-watchdog auto-blocked the task with reason "auto-blocked by zig-watchdog:
>=3 crashes detected" — even though run 102 produced ZERO crash events between
spawn and the watchdog's block.

Root cause: the M2 fix filters out `status=blocked` tasks but the diagnostic's
`consecutive_crashes` counter is a LIFETIME counter that Hermes does NOT reset
on unblock. When t_360fa078 went `blocked → unblocked → running`, the
diagnostic still showed `consecutive_crashes=97` from the pre-skill-fix era.
The watchdog probe at the next tick (status=running, count=97) classified
the task as actionable + over-threshold and re-blocked it mid-flight.

## Symptom

Run 102 of t_360fa078:
- spawned 04:42:38 (PID 3185413)
- worker log shows "Initializing agent..." (got past the skill error)
- no `crashed` event between spawn and block
- watchdog tick at ~04:45 auto-blocked it
- worker process killed mid-flight (pgrep returned nothing post-block)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Have a task with sticky `consecutive_crashes >= 3` lifetime diagnostic
2. Unblock the task: `hermes kanban unblock <tid>`
3. Trigger dispatch: `hermes kanban dispatch --json` (worker spawns)
4. Wait ≤5 min for the next zig-watchdog tick
5. Observe: task is auto-blocked by zig-watchdog despite zero new crashes

## Fix

In `local-models/zig-watchdog.py` `HermesGatewayJob.probe()`: after deriving
`_actionable_crash_loop_task_ids` from the diagnostic, query each task's
event log via `hermes kanban show <tid>` and count `crashed` events whose
timestamps fall AFTER the most recent `unblocked` event. Only if the recent
count meets `HERMES_CRASH_LOOP_THRESHOLD` is the task truly actionable.

New helper: `_count_crashes_since_last_unblock(show_text: str) -> int`
parses the events block via regex (event-line format
`[YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM] [optional 'run N'] event_name {data}`).

## Source of Report

Empirical observation from this session — push notification to user when
the regression caught a working Phase 2 worker mid-flight.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Regression test: task with all crashes BEFORE most recent unblock → OK
      (NOT actionable, even though lifetime counter is high)
- [ ] Regression test: task with ≥3 crashes AFTER most recent unblock → CRASH
      (still actionable; revive will block)
- [ ] Regression test: task never unblocked + ≥3 crashes total → CRASH
      (preserves current detection of a fresh crash-loop)
- [ ] Regression test: unparseable events → conservative fallback to lifetime
      counter (no false negatives)
- [ ] All existing zig-watchdog tests still pass (21/21)
- [ ] Bead: dotfiles-cke in commit trailer

title: mlx-lm: server response parser missing Mistral [TOOL_CALLS] envelope support
description:
## Problem

`mlx_lm.server` (verified at v0.31.3) has a tool-call response parser that recognizes OpenAI-style `<|tool_call|>` markers and Qwen3-specific variants, but NOT Mistral's `[TOOL_CALLS]function_name[ARGS]{json}` envelope. Any Mistral-family model (Devstral, Mistral-Small, Codestral, etc.) served via mlx_lm.server returns responses where the tool-call bytes are silently dropped from the message.

## Symptom

Request a tool call against a Mistral-envelope model:
```bash
curl -s -X POST http://...:8090/v1/chat/completions \
  -d '{"model":"devstral","messages":[...],"tools":[...],"tool_choice":"auto"}'
```

Response:
```json
{"finish_reason": "stop", "message": {"role": "assistant"}, "usage": {"completion_tokens": 44, ...}}
```

- No `content` key (would be there for plain text)
- No `tool_calls` key (would be there if parsed)
- 44 tokens generated but invisible — parser ate them

Direct CLI probe via `mlx_lm.generate --model lmstudio-community/Devstral-Small-2507-MLX-4bit ...` (bypassing the server) confirms the model emits `[TOOL_CALLS]Edit[ARGS]{...}` correctly. The issue is purely the server's response parser.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. SSH to a host with `mlx_lm.server` v0.31.3+ running
2. Load `lmstudio-community/Devstral-Small-2507-MLX-4bit` (or any Mistral-envelope model)
3. POST to `/v1/chat/completions` with `tools[]` populated and `tool_choice: "auto"` or `"required"`
4. Observe: response message has empty `role: "assistant"`, no `content`, no `tool_calls`, N completion tokens reported but invisible

Sanity check (proves decode pipeline works): same model, no `tools` in the request → returns proper `content: "..."` text response.

## Suspected cause

mlx_lm.server's `tool_call_parser` likely hard-codes regex for `<|tool_call|>...</|tool_call|>` or `<tool_call>...</tool_call>` envelope shapes (matches OpenAI/Qwen3). Mistral models emit `[TOOL_CALLS]name[ARGS]{json}` instead. Parser doesn't match → strips the text from content but doesn't surface as tool_calls either.

## Source of report

This session's `dotfiles-ukx.11` Devstral swap probe (2026-05-27). Full 3-layer breakdown at:
- `explore/local-coding-models/refs/research/research-devstral-mlx-server-parser-gap.md`
- `local-models/GUARDRAILS.md` G18

## Deferred — investigate before filing upstream

This bead is DEFERRED. Before filing an upstream issue at `ml-explore/mlx-lm`:

1. Confirm by reading mlx_lm.server source (look at the tool-call response parser in particular)
2. Check existing GitHub issues — someone may have already filed
3. Decide: file an issue, write a PR with a Mistral parser, OR write a local CCR transformer shim (pattern: `local-models/trinity_shim.py`)
4. Consider: is fixing this worth it given Devstral has compound problems at the model level too (serial-not-parallel emission + hallucinated schemas + tokenizer regex bug)? Qwen3-Coder via llama-swap is the canonical tool-tier path. Devstral was nice-to-have.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] mlx_lm.server source confirmed as the gap (read tool_call_parser, identify the regex/format-detection logic)
- [ ] Upstream issue searched (any open ones?)
- [ ] Decision: upstream PR / local shim / drop. Documented in --notes.
- [ ] If shim chosen: implement following `trinity_shim.py` pattern, verify via the ukx.9 curl recipe
notes:
DEFERRED 2026-06-06 via /triage pass. The bead description itself says 'DEFERRED — investigate before filing upstream' and lists work that requires deciding upstream-PR-vs-shim-vs-drop. Production is unblocked: Qwen3-Coder via llama-swap is the canonical tool-tier path; Devstral was nice-to-have and has compound model-level problems anyway (G18). Revisit if mlx_lm.server gains Mistral envelope support upstream OR if a Mistral-family model becomes uniquely valuable.

---
MOVED 2026-06-06 to local-coding-models-f19 (bead-location-discipline pass; mlx-server bugs belong in local-coding-models project tree, not dotfiles infra).

title: hooks: add post-gen-fanout-check.sh — gamma/openrouter fan-out reminder
description:
## Context
/gamma and /openrouter are cost-aware skills — one generation per call,
confirm cost first, never fan out variants on speculation. That
discipline is text-only. The "never fire autonomously" half depends on
user intent (unobservable to a hook), but the speculative-loop pattern
— several generations in quick succession — IS mechanically visible.

## Task
Add post-gen-fanout-check.sh, a PostToolUse:Bash hook. It counts
successful gamma-render.sh / openrouter-image.sh invocations per session
in a rolling 10-minute window and prints a one-line stderr reminder on
the 2nd+. WARN only — never blocks (the call already ran; the user may
legitimately want it). Wire it into settings.json.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] post-gen-fanout-check.sh created; PostToolUse:Bash, exits 0 always
- [ ] 2nd+ generation in the window prints a reminder; the 1st does not
- [ ] failed generations (exit_code != 0) not counted
- [ ] wired into settings.json PostToolUse Bash array

title: onboard: promote skill-loading to its own gated step
description:
## Context
When a user invokes /onboard, the agent unreliably reads all skill
bodies — it skips some, or delegates the read to an Explore agent whose
context is then discarded. The user has had to explicitly say "read all
skills directly, no Explore agent" every session. The skill already
said to do this, but as buried item 4 of Step 1, framed as an outcome
rather than a forcing procedure.

## Task
Promote skill-loading to its own numbered step (Step 2): an
enumerate-via-ls read list, an explicit forbidden-shortcuts list
(Explore-agent delegation / sampling / skip-because-long), and a
verification gate — the orientation report must state "Skills loaded:
N/N". Renumber subsequent steps and fix cross-references.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Skill-loading is its own step with a concrete ls procedure
- [ ] Forbidden shortcuts named explicitly
- [ ] Orientation report gates on the N/N count
- [ ] Step renumbering + cross-refs consistent
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] skill-loading is its own step with an ls procedure
 - [ ] forbidden shortcuts named; report gates on N/N count
 - [ ] step renumbering + cross-refs consistent

title: spec: phase 1 local coding-agent stack on pico (Qwen3-Coder + claude-code-router + Ollama)
description:
## 1. Overview

Phase 1 deployment of a Claude-Code-style coding agent backed by a local model on pico (Mac Studio M1 Max, 64 GB). Selects Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct as the model, Ollama as the server (current, with 3 env tweaks), claude-code-router as the protocol-translation proxy, and keeps the user on Claude Code as the daily-driver harness. Pi Coding Agent is installed alongside as the kernel for future custom-harness experiments.

What this spec does NOT cover (deferred to a Phase 2 spec):
- LM Studio / MLX A/B test for the 2× decode win
- Aider as the diff-format coding-quality fallback
- GLM-4.5-Air for "quality > speed" runs
- Phoenix observability hookup
- Bespoke per-use-case harnesses (chat / batch / etc.)

## 2. Current State / Baseline

Pico runs Ollama 0.24.0 via a custom LaunchAgent (`com.zig.ollama`). Current env (verified live 2026-05-25):

```
OLLAMA_HOST=100.72.47.4:11434     # tailnet bind, NOT loopback
OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1
OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE=q8_0
OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=24h
```

Service is healthy: cold model load ~15s, warm calls ~45-200ms TTFT, ~62 tok/s on the existing qwen2.5:7b. Pre-pulled models: `qwen2.5:7b` (4.6 GB base, NOT coding-tuned), `llama3.2:3b` (2.0 GB, too small for coding).

Plist source-of-truth: `~/dotfiles/ollama/com.zig.ollama.plist` with `TAILSCALE_IP_PLACEHOLDER` substituted at install time by `mac.setup.sh` lines 65-74.

Claude Code currently uses Anthropic-hosted Sonnet 4.6 — no local-backend wiring exists yet.

## 3. Changes and Decisions

| # | Change | Decision | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1 | Add `OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH=131072` | Single biggest fix. Default 4K silently truncates and breaks tool loops. 128K matches harnesses-agent recommendation and gives serious coding-session capacity | both serving + harnesses agents flagged independently |
| 3.2 | Add `OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=2` | 64 GB has headroom for 2 concurrent slots | serving agent |
| 3.3 | Add `OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS=2` | Allows primary + fallback (or embedding) resident simultaneously | serving agent |
| 3.4 | Pull `qwen3-coder:30b` | MoE 30B-A3B (3.3B active) — right architecture for M1 Max's bandwidth profile. 51.6% SWE-bench Verified, tool-calling fixed Aug 2025, ~40-55 tok/s on Ollama | models agent ranking |
| 3.5 | Install `claude-code-router` on pico | Translates Anthropic protocol → Ollama OpenAI-compat. CCR-on-pico keeps the chatty proxy↔model traffic on loopback; one tailnet hop for CC→CCR | harnesses agent |
| 3.6 | New LaunchAgent `com.zig.ccr` | Match existing zig-zone service discipline (own LaunchAgent so env vars don't get clobbered) | mac.setup.sh convention |
| 3.7 | Install Pi alongside (no LaunchAgent) | Pi is interactive CLI; install for experimentation + as fork kernel for bespoke harnesses | user directive + harnesses agent |
| 3.8 | CC client config: `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=http://100.72.47.4:<ccr-port>` | Single env var on the CC client side; reversible (unset to go back to hosted) | claude-code-router docs |

## 4. Formal Specification

### 4.1 Ollama env (plist update)

Target file: `~/dotfiles/ollama/com.zig.ollama.plist` (then re-deploy to pico).

Add to the existing `<dict>` under `EnvironmentVariables`:

```xml
<key>OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH</key><string>131072</string>
<key>OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL</key><string>2</string>
<key>OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS</key><string>2</string>
```

Re-deploy: copy plist to pico, `launchctl unload` + `launchctl load -w`. Verify env via `launchctl list com.zig.ollama` and a smoke test.

### 4.2 Model pull

On pico (or via tailnet API):
```
ollama pull qwen3-coder:30b
```

Expected: ~19 GB download. Disk check before: `df -h /opt/homebrew` (Ollama default model store) should show ≥30 GB free.

### 4.3 claude-code-router install

On pico:
```
brew install node       # if not present (pico's Phoenix path requires Python; check Node first)
npm install -g @musistudio/claude-code-router
mkdir -p ~/.config/claude-code-router
cp ~/dotfiles/ccr/config.json ~/.config/claude-code-router/config.json    # new dotfiles file
```

Config (`~/dotfiles/ccr/config.json` — new file in dotfiles):
- One provider entry pointing at `http://127.0.0.1:11434/v1` (Ollama OpenAI-compat endpoint)
- Routes: `default` → `qwen3-coder:30b`, `background` → `qwen3-coder:30b` (only one model in Phase 1)
- Bind: `0.0.0.0:3456` (will be tailnet-only via firewall, OR `100.72.47.4:3456` if CCR supports explicit bind)

### 4.4 New LaunchAgent: com.zig.ccr

New file: `~/dotfiles/ccr/com.zig.ccr.plist`. Same pattern as `com.zig.ollama.plist`:
- `Label`: `com.zig.ccr`
- `ProgramArguments`: `["/opt/homebrew/bin/ccr", "start"]` (or wherever the npm global binary lands)
- `EnvironmentVariables`: `HOST=100.72.47.4`, `PORT=3456` (or per CCR's env contract)
- `RunAtLoad: true`, `KeepAlive: true`
- `StandardOutPath` / `StandardErrorPath`: `/opt/homebrew/var/log/ccr.log`

### 4.5 mac.setup.sh additions

After the Ollama LaunchAgent block (~line 74 in current `mac.setup.sh`):

```bash
# --- claude-code-router (Phase 1 local-coding-agent proxy) ---
npm install -g @musistudio/claude-code-router
mkdir -p ~/.config/claude-code-router
sed "s/TAILSCALE_IP_PLACEHOLDER/$(tailscale ip -4)/" \
    ~/dotfiles/ccr/config.json \
    > ~/.config/claude-code-router/config.json
sed "s/TAILSCALE_IP_PLACEHOLDER/$(tailscale ip -4)/" \
    ~/dotfiles/ccr/com.zig.ccr.plist \
    > ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ccr.plist
launchctl load -w ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ccr.plist

# --- Pi Coding Agent (alongside CC, for experimentation + custom-harness reference) ---
# Pi is interactive CLI; no LaunchAgent. Install once; user invokes manually.
brew install pi-agent || npm install -g @pi-dev/pi   # verify which is canonical before shipping

# --- Pull primary coding model (one-time; subsequent re-runs skip if present) ---
ollama list | grep -q "qwen3-coder:30b" || ollama pull qwen3-coder:30b
```

### 4.6 CC client config

On whichever host runs CC (zig, metis, or both):
```
export ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=http://100.72.47.4:3456
```

Persist via shell init OR a CC project-local override. Reversible: unset to fall back to hosted Sonnet.

## 5. Test Cases (≥10)

```
TEST: env-vars-applied
INPUT:    launchctl list com.zig.ollama | grep -E 'CONTEXT_LENGTH|NUM_PARALLEL|MAX_LOADED'
EXPECTED: All 3 vars present with correct values
RATIONALE: Verifies plist redeploy worked

TEST: model-pulled
INPUT:    curl http://100.72.47.4:11434/api/tags | jq '.models[].name'
EXPECTED: includes "qwen3-coder:30b"
RATIONALE: Primary model is available

TEST: model-cold-load-under-30s
INPUT:    time curl -sS http://100.72.47.4:11434/api/generate -d '{"model":"qwen3-coder:30b","prompt":"print hi","stream":false}'
EXPECTED: completes in <30s wall-clock from cold
RATIONALE: Baseline for "is this usable at all" — model agent estimated 19 GB load

TEST: model-warm-tok-per-sec
INPUT:    (run generate twice; measure eval_count/eval_duration on the second)
EXPECTED: ≥30 tok/s warm
RATIONALE: Models agent estimated 40-55 tok/s; 30 is the floor below which CC tool loops would feel slow

TEST: ccr-process-running
INPUT:    ssh pico 'launchctl list com.zig.ccr'
EXPECTED: status = 0, PID present
RATIONALE: LaunchAgent is loaded and ccr binary is alive

TEST: ccr-anthropic-endpoint-responsive
INPUT:    curl -X POST http://100.72.47.4:3456/v1/messages -H "x-api-key: dummy" -H "anthropic-version: 2023-06-01" -d '{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5","max_tokens":10,"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"hi"}]}'
EXPECTED: 200 with a valid Anthropic-shape response body
RATIONALE: CCR translation layer works end-to-end

TEST: cc-end-to-end-no-tool
INPUT:    From CC client: ask a no-tool question ("what's the capital of France?")
EXPECTED: Coherent answer via the local model (Paris)
RATIONALE: Baseline end-to-end without tool-call complexity

TEST: cc-end-to-end-one-tool
INPUT:    From CC client: "list the files in /tmp"
EXPECTED: CC invokes ls tool, model returns a coherent file listing
RATIONALE: Tool-call fidelity (the #1 risk per both research agents)

TEST: cc-end-to-end-one-edit
INPUT:    From CC client: create a one-line file, then ask CC to edit it
EXPECTED: Edit completes; file content matches expected
RATIONALE: Multi-turn tool-use flow

TEST: context-not-truncated
INPUT:    Send a 50K-token prompt via CCR; check Ollama log for any "truncated" warning
EXPECTED: No truncation warning; full prompt processed
RATIONALE: Validates OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH=131072 actually took effect

TEST: pi-installed-and-launches
INPUT:    ssh pico 'pi --version'
EXPECTED: Version string, exit 0
RATIONALE: Pi alongside-install worked

TEST: ccr-survives-pico-reboot
INPUT:    Reboot pico; verify CCR is up via the anthropic-endpoint test
EXPECTED: Endpoint responsive within 60s of boot
RATIONALE: LaunchAgent KeepAlive + RunAtLoad correctness

TEST: cc-rollback-to-hosted
INPUT:    Unset ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL on CC client; retry the same prompt
EXPECTED: CC routes to hosted Anthropic Sonnet 4.6
RATIONALE: Reversibility — this is opt-in, not a one-way migration
```

## 6. Open Questions

- OQ-01 (P1): **Where does the CC client run** — only on zig, only on metis, or both? Affects whether ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL is set in dotfiles shell init (which propagates to both) or per-host. *Recommendation: set per-host via shell init; CC project-local override available for any project that should always use hosted Sonnet.*
- OQ-02 (P1): **What's the CCR config schema** for routing per "agent / planner / background" model slots? Phase 1 only has one model, so all routes point at qwen3-coder:30b — but the schema needs to be right for Phase 2 to add Devstral/GLM cleanly. *Recommendation: read CCR's docs at impl time; design Phase 1 config to be Phase-2-extensible.*
- OQ-03 (P2): **Does CCR bind to a specific IP** or only 0.0.0.0? If 0.0.0.0, need to confirm pico's firewall blocks 3456 from non-tailnet sources (pf rules or macOS firewall config). *Recommendation: check CCR env contract first; if no explicit bind, document the firewall step.*
- OQ-04 (P2): **Pi install path** — `brew install pi-agent` vs `npm install -g @pi-dev/pi` vs `gh repo clone pi-dev/pi && bun install`. Verify canonical install before shipping mac.setup.sh edits. *Recommendation: read Pi's README at impl time.*
- OQ-05 (P3): **OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE — keep q8_0 or revisit?** For a 19 GB model + 128K context + Q8 KV, memory budget is tight. Serving agent said keep q8_0; but if context-length increase causes pressure, may need to drop to q4_0 KV. *Recommendation: keep q8_0 in Phase 1; benchmark memory footprint as part of testing; downshift only if OOM.*
- OQ-06 (P3): **Do we want an embedding model resident** (e.g. `nomic-embed-text`) for CC retrieval-style features? Phase 2 candidate. *Recommendation: defer to Phase 2; MAX_LOADED_MODELS=2 leaves the slot.*

## 7. Future Considerations

Phase 2 spec (deferred, separate bead):
- LM Studio + MLX A/B test (the 2× decode win on this model class)
- Devstral-Small-2 (24B) as fallback when Qwen3-Coder loops
- GLM-4.5-Air (106B MoE, 4-bit MLX) for "quality > iteration speed" runs
- Aider integration as the diff-format coding-quality fallback
- Phoenix observability hookup (CCR or Ollama as OTel source)
- One bespoke thin harness as a proof — likely chat (`simonw/llm`) or batch eval (pydantic-ai)
- Embedding model for CC-style retrieval (`nomic-embed-text`)

Phase 3 (longer horizon):
- Ollama MLX backend (preview in 0.19+) when stable — closes the MLX gap with zero migration
- Custom-harness paragon skill if the pattern stabilizes (i.e. we have 2-3 successful bespoke harnesses)
- Cross-model routing in CCR config (planner = GLM, executor = Qwen3-Coder, background = Devstral)

 - [ ] mac.setup.sh updated so a fresh pico bring-up gets the full stack
 - [ ] Reversibility verified (ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL unset returns to hosted Sonnet)

title: task: A/B test Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B on Ollama vs MLX (the primary)
description:
## Context
Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct is the spec's Phase 1 primary model (dotfiles-enx.4). MoE architecture (3.3B active params); models agent estimated 40-55 tok/s on Ollama and 55-75 tok/s on MLX based on M2 Max extrapolation — needs real M1 Max measurement. Pre-req: dotfiles-enx.5 (MLX install).

## Task
Run identical benchmark protocol against the SAME model on both stacks:

### Setup
- Ollama: `ollama pull qwen3-coder:30b` (already in spec)
- MLX: download from Hugging Face (Qwen ships official MLX weights at `mlx-community/Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct-4bit` typically; verify)

### Benchmark suite (run on BOTH; capture raw numbers)
1. **Cold load**: time from request → first byte for a freshly-spawned process
2. **Warm TTFT**: 10 short prompts, measure ms to first byte
3. **Sustained decode**: 1000-token completion of a coding prompt, measure tokens/sec
4. **Tool-call success rate**: 20 representative agentic prompts (suite to design — mix of file-read, edit, multi-step), measure JSON-valid tool calls
5. **Long context**: 50K-token prompt, does each truncate? error? succeed?
6. **Memory footprint**: idle, model-loaded, mid-generation
7. **24h stability**: optional — loop probe for crash/hang behavior

### Test prompt suite location
Save the 20-prompt agentic suite to `~/explore/local-coding-models/refs/coding-eval-prompts.md` so subsequent model A/Bs (Devstral, GLM-4.5-Air) use the same prompts.

## Output
`~/explore/local-coding-models/refs/qwen3-coder-ollama-vs-mlx.md` with raw measurements + verdict (which stack wins for THIS model on M1 Max). Updates dotfiles-enx.4 --notes with the empirical finding.

## Acceptance
- [ ] Model running on both stacks
- [ ] 20-prompt eval suite written + saved to refs/
- [ ] Full benchmark run on both; raw numbers captured
- [ ] qwen3-coder-ollama-vs-mlx.md written with verdict
- [ ] dotfiles-enx.4 --notes updated

title: task: GLM-4.5-Air feasibility + A/B on 64GB (the quality ceiling)
description:
## Context
GLM-4.5-Air (106B-A12B MoE, 4-bit MLX, ~55-58GB) is the smartest model that physically fits in 64GB unified memory per the models agent — 57.6% SWE-bench Verified. Tight fit (needs KV quantization + context discipline) and only ~8-15 tok/s, so it's for "quality > iteration speed" runs, not the daily driver.

Lower priority than Qwen3-Coder + Devstral A/Bs since:
1. It may not even be available on Ollama (Z.ai's GLM weights ship MLX-first via mlx-community)
2. Memory budget is tight — may need to disable other LaunchAgents temporarily for the test
3. Slow enough that 24/7 use is unlikely; this is a "switch to it for hard problems" model

## Task
1. **Feasibility check first**: does Ollama have GLM-4.5-Air in its library? If MLX-only, that's a real finding — it forces stack choice for this model alone.
2. **Memory budget check**: with mlx-lm + 4-bit GLM-4.5-Air + the existing Phoenix/A1111/Postgres/Ollama on pico, do we hit OOM? Plan: stop A1111 during the test (it's the biggest memory consumer); keep Ollama running but unload its model.
3. **If feasible**: run the same 20-prompt eval suite as dotfiles-enx.6 + .7.
4. **If only MLX**: just benchmark MLX, note that "Ollama" half of A/B is N/A.

## Output
`~/explore/local-coding-models/refs/glm-4.5-air-feasibility.md` covering: availability matrix (Ollama y/n, MLX y/n), memory-budget findings, benchmark results (where applicable), verdict on whether to keep it as a quality-tier model.

## Acceptance
- [ ] Availability matrix populated for both stacks
- [ ] Memory budget verified (no OOM, or documented workaround)
- [ ] Benchmark run on the supported stack(s)

description:

title: tailnet acl: add Taildrop file-sharing grant (bi-directional server↔user)
description:
## Context

Discovered while delivering `stable-diffusion-handoff.md` to the iPhone
via `tailscale file cp /tmp/stable-diffusion-handoff.md iphone-15-pro:`:
the command refused with `cannot send files: peer is owned by a
different user`. Tailscale's default-deny ACL treats Taildrop as a
per-user feature — devices owned by the same user can drop to each
other, but cross-owner (and tag→user) transfers are blocked by default.

zig-computer + pico are tagged `tag:server` (machine identities, no
user). iPhone + metis are owned by `azigler@`. Without a grant, no
file-sharing between them.

## Fix

Added a `grants` block to `tailscale/acl.jsonc` using the
`tailscale.com/cap/file-sharing` capability. Combined src+dst arrays
cover the full server↔user matrix in one rule. Capability-only grant
(no L3 traffic implication beyond what existing acls allow).

After applying via Tailscale admin console paste:
```bash
tailscale file cp <file> iphone-15-pro:    # tag:server → user
tailscale file cp <file> zig-computer:     # user → tag:server (from metis / iPhone)
```

## Acceptance criteria

- [x] `tailscale/acl.jsonc` updated with `grants` block
- [x] Comment block documents the rationale + capability syntax
- [ ] User pastes updated acl.jsonc into Tailscale admin console
- [ ] `tailscale file cp /tmp/stable-diffusion-handoff.md iphone-15-pro:`
      succeeds from zig-computer (verification target)
- [ ] Inverse direction tested optionally (drop from iPhone Tailscale
      app to zig-computer)
notes:
## CLOSED 2026-05-24 — WON'T FIX (Tailscale design limitation)

Tailscale's admin policy editor rejected the grants block with:
"Error: capability name must not be in the tailscale.com domain" —
the namespace is reserved for built-in, non-user-configurable features.

Confirmed via Tailscale docs (kb/1106/taildrop): "Taildrop is currently
limited to sending files between your own personal devices. You cannot
send files to devices owned by other users (even users on the same
Tailscale network)." Hard-locked by design; no ACL grant, nodeAttr,
or admin toggle.

Workarounds documented in zig-zone runbook gotcha #23:
- HTTP over tailnet IP (one-off): `python3 -m http.server` bound to
  `tailscale ip -4`, fetch from any tailnet device
- SSH relay through a user-owned tailnet device (scp → device A,
  Taildrop → device B)
- Untag the source (re-auth as user, breaks the identity model)

ACL revert pushed; comment block updated to document the limitation
so future-me doesn't re-try.

title: ccr: pin to 1.0.73 — v2.0.0 has regression (routing returns 'Provider undefined' for all requests)
description:
## Problem
@musistudio/claude-code-router v2.0.0 (released 2026-05-25, npm-default
since) has a routing regression: every request returns 404 'Provider
"undefined" not found' regardless of model name, request shape, or
endpoint. The Router.default / Router.background / etc. config is read
(providers register successfully at startup) but the request handler
can't resolve them.

Downgrade to 1.0.73 (last 1.x) restores full routing.

## Steps to Reproduce on 2.0.0
1. npm install -g @musistudio/claude-code-router@2.0.0
2. ccr restart
3. curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:3456/v1/messages \
     -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
     -H 'anthropic-version: 2023-06-01' \
     -d '{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ok"}],"max_tokens":5}'
4. Returns 404 'Provider "undefined" not found' (any model name fails)

## Fix
- npm install -g @musistudio/claude-code-router@1.0.73
- ccr restart
- Avoid `npm install -g @musistudio/claude-code-router` (latest) until 2.x is verified

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CCR pinned to 1.0.73 (current state — DONE 2026-06-01 this session)
- [ ] Note in dotfiles README or CLAUDE.md warning against 2.x upgrade
- [ ] When 2.x routing regression resolves upstream, re-evaluate
