title: zig-zone Phase 4: SS14 game server + watchdog → pico (with feasibility test)
acceptance_criteria:
- [x] SS14 game server runs natively on pico ARM64 (no qemu)
 - [x] SS14.Watchdog runs natively on pico ARM64
 - [x] Client connects through nginx UDP+TCP stream proxy on zig-computer
 - [x] tcpdump verified: client never sees pico's tailnet IP
 - [x] Gameplay works (player tested, admin commands work, postgres writes work)
 - [x] ss14-watchdog systemd unit disabled on zig-computer
 - [x] ss14-replay-rotate timer moved to pico (8th of 8)
 - [x] zig-computer nginx simplified to single proxy / → pico:8080
 - [x] /instances/, /watchdog/, /client.zip routes added to pico nginx
notes:
REVISED CONCLUSION 2026-05-24: SS14 ARM64 build technically works (validated end-to-end with a real player connection), but the deployment model is fundamentally incompatible with reverse-proxied UDP. Per the canonical SS14 architecture (dotfiles-ier research), the game server must live with a public IP that the launcher dials UDP directly. nginx stream{} proxying inverts what Lidgren expects of UDP source addresses. Two intrinsic bugs surfaced: (A) player counter pinned at 0/30 because every client appears as zig-computer's tailnet IP; (B) Lidgren disconnects from Late MsgEntity messages because nginx demuxes UDP into the wrong upstream socket.

ROLLBACK SHIPPED: SS14 game server + watchdog + ss14-replay-rotate timer restored to zig-computer. ARM64 build artifacts preserved on pico (/Users/pico/ss14-watchdog/) in case a future rearchitecture (home-router DNAT for direct UDP, TPROXY, or VPN-based source-preservation) makes SS14-on-pico viable.

Net Phase 4 outcome: SS14 + ss14-watchdog + ss14-replay-rotate STAY on zig-computer permanently. This is a single intentional exception to 'all hostable moves to pico' — justified because zig-computer naturally owns the public IPv4 and SS14's game-server architecture requires that co-location. M1 Max's compute advantages don't help SS14 (latency-bound, not GPU-bound); they DO help the 99% of compute (postgres, LLM, web, builds, obs) which IS on pico.

Spec's Phase 4 wording was sound — the feasibility test result was 'fail'; the spec correctly said 'abort SS14 migration. SS14 stays on zig-computer.'

title: fix: zig-watchdog HermesGateway probe surfaces stale blocked-task crash diagnostics as CRASH + service Documentation= invalid URL
description:
## Problem

Two cosmetic-but-real issues with the freshly-shipped zig-watchdog.

### Bug 1 — sticky HermesGateway=CRASH false positive

The HermesGateway probe matches `repeated_crashes` diagnostics from
`hermes kanban diagnostics` to detect crash-loops. Once a task is already
blocked (manually OR by the watchdog's own revive), the diagnostic counter
stays attached to the task forever — the gateway never clears it on block.
Result: every watchdog tick reports HermesGateway=CRASH and the dashboard
shows a red banner — even though the gateway is healthy.

This is the M2 minor finding from the /scrutinize pass on dotfiles-olh.

### Bug 2 — invalid Documentation= URL

`zig-watchdog.service` line 3 had `Documentation=https://... (bead dotfiles-olh)`.
systemd parses Documentation= as space-separated URLs; the parenthetical
fails URL validation, producing two journal warnings per service start.

## Steps to Reproduce

### Bug 1
1. Have any task with `repeated_crashes` diagnostic AND status=blocked
   (today: t_360fa078 with 97x consecutive_crashes, blocked)
2. Confirm gateway is healthy: `systemctl --user is-active hermes-gateway` → active
3. Run `python3 ~/dotfiles/local-models/zig-watchdog.py`
4. Read `~/.cache/zig-watchdog/state.json` → HermesGateway.last_probe is CRASH
5. Expected: OK (gateway healthy, blocked task is not actionable)

### Bug 2
1. Install zig-watchdog timer: `bash ~/dotfiles/local-models/install-zig-watchdog.sh`
2. `journalctl --user -u zig-watchdog.service --since '2 minutes ago' | grep "Invalid URL"`
3. Two "Invalid URL, ignoring:" lines per service start

## Fix

### Bug 1
In `local-models/zig-watchdog.py` `HermesGatewayJob.probe()`: return CRASH only
when `_actionable_crash_loop_task_ids` (already populated, filters out blocked
tasks) is non-empty. Stale blocked-task diagnostics → OK.

### Bug 2
In `local-models/zig-watchdog.service` Documentation= field: drop the
parenthetical; keep just the URL. Bead reference belongs in commit history,
not in a systemd field that requires URL syntax.

## Source of Report

User noticed dashboard showing "Hermes gateway is in a crashing loop" —
investigation showed gateway healthy but watchdog probe surfacing stale
diagnostic. Plus this session's earlier scrutinize M2 callout flagged it.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Bug 1: HermesGatewayJob.probe() returns OK when only blocked-task crash diagnostics exist
- [ ] Bug 1: Regression test: probe with only-blocked-task crashes → OK
- [ ] Bug 1: Regression test: probe with actionable (non-blocked) task crashes → still CRASH
- [ ] Bug 2: systemd journal shows zero "Invalid URL" warnings after service restart
- [ ] All existing zig-watchdog tests still pass
- [ ] Bead: dotfiles-hpi in commit trailer
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Bug 1: HermesGatewayJob.probe() returns OK when only blocked-task crash diagnostics exist
 - [ ] Bug 1: Regression test: probe with only-blocked-task crashes → OK
 - [ ] Bug 1: Regression test: probe with actionable (non-blocked) task crashes → still CRASH
 - [ ] Bug 2: systemd journal shows zero Invalid URL warnings after service restart
 - [ ] All existing zig-watchdog tests still pass (21/21)
 - [ ] Bead: dotfiles-hpi in commit trailer

title: ss14 player count stuck at 0 behind nginx UDP reverse proxy (Lidgren source-IP collapse)
description:
## Context
zig-zone Phase 4 (dotfiles-hdo) moved SS14 game server + watchdog to pico (Mac Studio M1 Max), reached from the public IP via `nginx stream{}` UDP+TCP proxy on zig-computer. Game traffic on `:1212` (Lidgren netcode UDP + TCP /info). The proxy chain:

```
Client (172.x.x.x:50870) →UDP→ zig-computer (51.81.33.136:1212, nginx stream)
                          →UDP→ tailnet (100.98.174.21:39088 → 100.72.47.4:1212)
                          →UDP→ pico Robust.Server (*:1212)
```

Verified working with tcpdump — client's real IP is hidden behind nginx; from pico's perspective every UDP packet arrives with source `100.98.174.21:<ephemeral>` (zig-computer tailnet IP).

## Bugs (two — likely shared root cause)

### Bug A: Player counter shows 0/30 in launcher even with active players
SS14 launcher's server browser polls TCP `/info` which reports `players: 0/30` while actual `spacezig` user is connected and walking around in-game. Connection counter never increments. Admin commands work; postgres writes work; game state syncs.

### Bug B: Random disconnects with "Late MsgEntity! Diff: -4250" warnings
Server logs show:
```
[INFO] net: Approved "100.98.174.21:52905" with username "spacezig"
[WARN] net.ent: Got late MsgEntity! Diff: -4250, msgT: 2102, cT: 6352, player: spacezig
```
Diff is in milliseconds — packets arriving 4+ seconds late. Client times out and disconnects after enough late messages.

## Probable shared root cause
nginx UDP stream proxy collapses ALL upstream UDP into a single "session" keyed by client source IP+port from the proxy's perspective. From Robust.Server's view, every packet looks like it comes from `100.98.174.21` — players never get distinguishable per-IP tracking. Likely Robust.Server:
- Uses source IP+port as the player identity, so multiple players collide
- Counts unique source IPs (proxy-IP-aware code excludes? probably not)
- Reorders / dedupes packets in ways that mismatch Lidgren's expected stream

## Research the subagent should do
The user says: "we are likely not the only SS14 server behind a reverse proxy, so this filtering hypothesis might not be real." Spot on — many SS14 community servers run cloudflare / load balancer / k8s in front. Find the canonical solution.

1. **SS14 / RobustToolbox docs**: search `docs.spacestation14.com` + RobustToolbox README/docs for "reverse proxy", "PROXY protocol", "real_ip", "trusted_proxies", "X-Forwarded-For" (over UDP — unlikely but check), "Lidgren NAT". Production SS14 fork operators must have hit this.
2. **GitHub search**: `github.com/space-wizards/space-station-14` + `RobustToolbox` issues + discussions for "behind reverse proxy", "player count 0", "Late MsgEntity proxy", "source IP collapse", "PROXY protocol UDP", "Lidgren proxy".
3. **nginx config patterns**: nginx supports `proxy_protocol` on `stream{}` to forward original client IP as v1/v2 PROXY header. Does Robust.Server's Lidgren transport support parsing PROXY headers? If yes, that's the fix. If not, find what other community SS14 ops have done.
4. **Lidgren-specific options**: Lidgren has cvars for source-port handling. Check `net.tickrate`, `net.maxconnections`, `net.allowed_addresses`, NAT-related options. Maybe there's a "trust X CIDR as source-stripper" option.
5. **Alternative architectures used by other SS14 hosts**: maybe they run nginx-on-pico binding the public IP via wireguard / tailscale subnet route / bgp / something instead of the zig-computer-proxy pattern. If the canonical answer is "don't proxy UDP", document that and we'll architecturally rethink.

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Root cause identified for both Bug A (counter 0/30) and Bug B (Late MsgEntity disconnects)
 - [ ] Concrete fix proposed (config change, code patch, architectural shift) with citation
 - [ ] If fix is PROXY protocol: confirmed whether Robust.Server's Lidgren transport supports parsing
 - [ ] If fix is architectural: describe the alternate pattern with pros/cons vs current setup
 - [ ] Report includes at least 3 cited sources (SS14 docs, GitHub issues, community forum posts, etc.)
notes:
FOLLOWUP RESOLUTION (research agent round 2, 2026-05-24): the counter bug was independent of the UDP proxy. Direct quote from Content.Server/GameTicking/GameTicker.StatusShell.cs:47-49 —
  jObject[\"players\"] = _cfg.GetCVar(CCVars.AdminsCountInReportedPlayerCount)
      ? _playerManager.PlayerCount
      : _playerManager.PlayerCount - _adminManager.ActiveAdmins.Count();
cvar admin.admins_count_in_playercount defaults to false (Content.Shared/CCVar/CCVars.Admin.cs:171). spacezig is admin AND only player → 1-1=0. Hub-advert convention per upstream — admins shouldn't inflate their own server's apparent population.

FIX: added [admin] admins_count_in_playercount = true to /opt/ss14-watchdog/instances/vacation-station/config.toml on zig-computer. Robust.Server bounced to pick up.

Two separate issues investigated and resolved:
- Bug B (Late MsgEntity disconnects): real, fixed via SS14 rollback to zig-computer (canonical SS14 architecture: don't UDP-proxy)
- Bug A (counter 0/30): unrelated; admin-subtract default; cvar flip

title: ss14-wrapper: handle PROXY-header per-session (not per-datagram) — nginx emits once per src-tuple
description:
## Context

Caught during the live cutover (dotfiles-oxe) when the user tried to connect via SS14 launcher: handshake initiated then "Connection timed out". Diagnosed via nginx error log + tcpdump.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Cutover live SS14 traffic to nginx stream `:1212 → pico:11214` with `proxy_protocol on` (nginx 1.28.0)
2. Open SS14 launcher → connect to ss14://ss14.zig.computer
3. Lobby handshake begins; first Lidgren UDP packet arrives at wrapper WITH PROXY-v1 header (parsed correctly, session created with real client IP)
4. Subsequent handshake datagrams from the SAME `<src-ip, src-port>` tuple arrive at wrapper WITHOUT PROXY headers (because nginx UDP stream module's `proxy_protocol on` emits the header ONCE per session, not per datagram)
5. Wrapper's UDP loop re-parses every datagram independently — calls into `ParseProxyHeader` which fails with `proxy protocol signature not present` → datagram dropped
6. Lidgren handshake stalls → "Connection timed out" in launcher

## Cause

**The /check OQ-03 finding ("nginx emits PROXY v1 on every datagram") was wrong.** It was based on a synthetic test that sent 4 datagrams from DIFFERENT source ports — each creating a new nginx UDP "session," each getting its own PROXY header. In real Lidgren traffic, one client uses ONE source port for many handshake datagrams → nginx tracks as ONE session → PROXY header emitted ONCE.

The spec (dotfiles-9g1 §3.8) was updated post-/check to reflect this misunderstanding, and the wrapper impl (dotfiles-1bi proxy.go ServeUDP) was written to match. So both spec and impl have the bug.

## Fix

The wrapper's UDP loop needs to track source-tuple → session mapping and tolerate missing PROXY headers on subsequent datagrams:

```go
// Pseudocode for the fix in wrapper/proxy.go ServeUDP:
header, err := proxyproto.Read(reader)
if err != nil {
    // PROXY header not present — look up existing session by source tuple
    sess, ok := store.LookupBySrcTuple(srcAddr)
    if !ok {
        // No header AND no existing session — genuinely malformed
        droppedCounter.Inc()
        return
    }
    // Use the existing session; original payload IS the entire datagram (no header to strip)
    sess.FanOut.WriteTo(buf, sess.UpstreamAddr)
    return
}
// (existing first-packet path: parse header, create-or-reuse session, strip header, forward)
```

Plus `SessionStore` needs a `LookupBySrcTuple(srcAddr) (*Session, bool)` method (the existing `LookupByPort` lookups by fanout port, not source tuple).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `LookupBySrcTuple` added to SessionStore (with thread-safe lookup)
- [ ] UDP loop's parse-failure branch consults LookupBySrcTuple before dropping
- [ ] Existing unit tests still pass (35 from qts + 30 in vs14 from C# patches)
- [ ] New unit test: "subsequent datagram from same src-tuple without PROXY header routes via existing session"
- [ ] Integration test (ss14-wrapper/integration_test.sh) extended to send 3 datagrams from SAME src port; assert all 3 reach upstream
- [ ] Spec dotfiles-9g1 §3.8 updated to reflect "PROXY on first datagram per session, NOT per datagram"
- [ ] Re-scrutinize before re-cutover
- [ ] Re-run dotfiles-oxe cutover after fix lands; ban_address verification (Step 9) confirms real-IP capture works for actual gameplay (not just handshake)

## Related

- dotfiles-oxe (cutover, paused mid-execution; ROLLED BACK to zig; full rollback note in oxe --notes)
- dotfiles-1bi (original wrapper impl — has the bug)
- dotfiles-9g1 (spec — has the wrong §3.8 description; needs post-fix correction)
- /check dotfiles-9cj (OQ-03 decision that over-generalized the test result)
notes:
## Investigation + fix (subagent dotfiles-jc5)

### Root cause confirmed
ServeUDP's parse-failure branch (proxy.go ~line 148) unconditionally
`continue`s on ANY ParseProxyHeader error. For real nginx-UDP-session
traffic, the first datagram carries the PROXY header (session created
fine) but subsequent datagrams from the same client <src-ip, src-port>
arrive headerless — proxyproto.Read returns ErrNoProxyProtocol, the
wrapper logs "malformed PROXY header" and drops. Lidgren handshake
stalls → "Connection timed out".

The existing /check synthetic test sent datagrams from DIFFERENT
source ports (TEST-PAYLOAD-3A from 55556, TEST-PAYLOAD-3B from 55556
via separate nc invocations — each got a NEW nginx-side ephemeral
src port: sniffer saw 39506 vs 45807). Each was a new nginx-session
so each got a PROXY header → bug didn't manifest.

Wrapper-side loopback test confirms: nginx with `proxy_responses 0`
on loopback allocates a fresh upstream socket per datagram. To
exercise the regression deterministically (without depending on
nginx's per-version session semantics), the integration test bypasses
nginx for the regression stage and sends directly to the wrapper port.

### Fix shape
1. **session.go**: added `LookupBySrcTuple(publicSrc) (*Session, bool)`.
   Takes write lock, bumps LastSeen, returns existing session (or
   miss). Thread-safe with existing LookupOrCreate / LookupByPort.

2. **proxy.go**:
   - New sentinel `ErrNoProxyHeader` (distinct from `ErrMalformedProxyHeader`)
     to distinguish "no signature present" from "signature present but
     corrupt." `ParseProxyHeader` wraps `proxyproto.ErrNoProxyProtocol`
     as `ErrNoProxyHeader`; any other parseErr remains
     `ErrMalformedProxyHeader`.
   - `ServeUDP` parse-failure branch now switches on `errors.Is(err,
     ErrNoProxyHeader)`: if true and `store.LookupBySrcTuple(srcAddr)`
     hits, forward the RAW datagram (buf[:n], no header to strip) via
     the existing session's fan-out. If no session, drop (orphan).
     Other errors (truly malformed) → drop with metric as before.

### Regression guards
- **Unit**: `TestServeUDP_HeaderlessFollowupRoutesViaSession` —
  stands up real ServeUDP + synthetic upstream, sends 1 PROXY-headered
  datagram + 2 headerless follow-ups from same pinned src port,
  asserts ALL 3 land at upstream. Verified FAILS on pre-fix
  (stashed fix, kept this test, ran: payloads 2+3 never arrived).
- **Unit**: `TestServeUDP_HeaderlessOrphanIsDropped` — confirms the
  miss path (headerless datagram, no prior session) still drops.
- **Unit**: `TestSessionStore_LookupBySrcTuple_{Hit,Miss,BumpsLastSeen}`
  — covers the new SessionStore method directly.
- **Unit**: `TestParseProxyHeader_NoHeader_IsErrNoProxyHeader` +
  `TestParseProxyHeader_TruncatedHeader_IsErrMalformed` — pin the
  error-classification contract that ServeUDP depends on.
- **Integration**: `integration_test.sh` extended with new stage
  "dotfiles-jc5 regression: header-once + 2 headerless follow-ups
  direct to wrapper" — bypasses nginx (necessary on loopback) and
  asserts all 3 payloads + that UDS LOOKUP returns the synthetic
  REAL CLIENT IP from the FIRST datagram's PROXY header (proves
  the session was reused). Verified FAILS on pre-fix wrapper binary
  (only datagram 1 reached echo).

### Existing tests rewrites
None needed. The pre-existing wrapper tests (35 in qts wave) all
test `ParseProxyHeader` in isolation OR test SessionStore in
isolation. None asserted "every datagram must have a PROXY header"
at the ServeUDP level — that incorrect assumption only lived in
the docstring comment of ServeUDP itself (now corrected to describe
the three-branch routing).

Updated docstring in `session.go` `LookupOrCreate` to remove the
stale "TC03 — nginx emits PROXY header on every datagram" comment
(spec misunderstanding) and replace with a more honest description
(reuse path is rare in practice; common path is LookupBySrcTuple).

### Spec correction needed (NOT in this PR — orchestrator route to spec agent)
**dotfiles-9g1 §3.8 needs revision.** Current language says nginx
emits the PROXY header on EVERY UDP datagram (derived from the
/check OQ-03 synthetic test that used different src ports). The
correct statement is:

  "nginx's `stream { proxy_protocol on; }` emits the PROXY-v1
   header ONCE per nginx-UDP-session, where a session is keyed by
   client <src-ip, src-port> and persists for `proxy_timeout`.
   Subsequent datagrams from the same client tuple arrive at the
   upstream WITHOUT a PROXY header — the upstream must maintain
   session state keyed on the wrapper-side src tuple (= nginx's
   outbound socket addr) to route them."

Also TC03 in §5 (test catalog) needs renaming/clarifying: the
"second datagram from same 5-tuple" case is now exercised by
both LookupOrCreate (header-present reuse, rare in practice) AND
LookupBySrcTuple (headerless follow-up, the common path).

The /check decision `dotfiles-9cj` OQ-03 conclusion was wrong; a
follow-up /check or spec rev should explicitly retract it and link
to this bead as the post-cutover diagnosis.

### Test counts
- Pre-fix: 35 wrapper unit tests (42 with sub-tests via t.Run)
- Post-fix: 42 wrapper unit tests (58 with sub-tests)
  - +3 session: LookupBySrcTuple Hit/Miss/BumpsLastSeen
  - +2 proxy: NoHeader_IsErrNoProxyHeader, TruncatedHeader_IsErrMalformed
  - +2 proxy: ServeUDP_HeaderlessFollowupRoutesViaSession + HeaderlessOrphanIsDropped
- `go test -race -count=1 ./...` clean. `gofmt -l wrapper/` clean.
  `golangci-lint run ./wrapper/...` 0 issues.
- integration_test.sh passes end-to-end (rollback clean, SS14 :1212
  undisturbed throughout).

Dependencies:
  -> dotfiles-oxe (parent-child) - ss14-c2-cutover: 11-step playbook — flip SS14 from zig-computer to pico via wrapper
## Scrutiny — 2026-05-25 — SHIP

Adversarial reviewer (read-only, general-purpose subagent) independently reproduced
the pre-fix failure mode: reverted only the ServeUDP parse-failure branch body,
reran TestServeUDP_HeaderlessFollowupRoutesViaSession — only datagram 1 (with
header) reached upstream; datagrams 2 + 3 (headerless follow-ups) dropped at the
old unconditional log+continue. Fix restored → all 3 land. The regression guard
is real, not just additive.

**Verdict: SHIP.** Two Low-severity findings (non-blocking):

- L1. `ss14-wrapper/integration_test.sh:698-711` — secondary LOOKUP check is
  observational only (logs "INFO" instead of fail+exit on mismatch). The
  load-bearing `JC5_HITS != 3 → exit 1` assertion at 680-689 IS real.
- L2. `wrapper/proxy.go:126,199` — docstring mentions "drop with metric" but no
  counter exists in the codebase (pre-existing aspirational language carried
  forward from TC18 framing; not a jc5 regression).

**Verified by reviewer:** thread-safety of LookupBySrcTuple (write-lock covers
both map read + LastSeen mutation; race detector clean); three-branch parse
classification (ErrNoProxyHeader vs ErrMalformedProxyHeader); all 42 tests / 58
sub-tests pass with -race -count=1; new tests exercise the real ServeUDP loop
(not mocked); commit diff shows zero deletions in existing test bodies.

**Out-of-scope follow-ups (NOT jc5's gate):**

- Spec dotfiles-9g1 §3.8 correction — separate spec-agent dispatch
- /check decision dotfiles-9cj OQ-03 retraction — append supersession note
- dotfiles-oxe cutover re-run — that's the next-action, post-merge

title: tmux/com.zig.tmux.plist: replace hardcoded 'pico' with HOSTNAME_PLACEHOLDER (fixup)
description:
## Problem
Just-committed `tmux/com.zig.tmux.plist` (commit 7ec840c, bead dotfiles-s4t) has `<string>pico</string>` hardcoded as the tmux session name. `mac.setup.sh` was already updated to do `sed s/HOSTNAME_PLACEHOLDER/$(hostname -s)/` at install time, expecting the placeholder token — but the actual plist file shipped with the wrong literal.

Caused by a tool-use ordering miss: I tried to Edit the plist after the cp without first reading it (the Edit tool requires prior Read of the file). The Edit silently failed; the rest of the commit went through with the original-from-cp content.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Fresh clone dotfiles on a Mac with hostname != "pico"
2. Run `bash mac.setup.sh`
3. `sed s/HOSTNAME_PLACEHOLDER/<hostname>/` finds NO matches in the plist (the placeholder isn't there)
4. Result: `/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.tmux.plist` still has session name = "pico" on every Mac that runs the script

## Fix
Replace `<string>pico</string>` with `<string>HOSTNAME_PLACEHOLDER</string>` in `tmux/com.zig.tmux.plist`. `mac.setup.sh` already does the sed substitution correctly.

## Regression guard
Manual visual check on the committed plist. (No automated test exists for dotfile content; this is a one-off pre-commit-discipline miss.)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] `tmux/com.zig.tmux.plist` line 13 reads `<string>HOSTNAME_PLACEHOLDER</string>` not `<string>pico</string>`
- [x] `mac.setup.sh` sed substitution remains correct (it was already right)

title: ss14-gre: test GRE/IP-in-IP tunnel between zig-computer and pico for SS14
description:
## Context

Sub-bead of `dotfiles-52c` (explore: SS14-on-pico via TPROXY or GRE —
preserve source IPs + VPS-IP public face). This bead is the **GRE / IP-
in-IP tunnel** test track. See parent for full rationale.

## Goal

Validate (or rigorously disprove) that an IP-in-IP tunnel between
zig-computer (Linux) and pico (macOS) can deliver SS14 packets to pico
with real client source IPs intact, by encapsulating entire packets
end-to-end through the tunnel. Zig-computer remains the only publicly-
known address.

## Phase 1 research findings (2026-05-24)

- **macOS gif(4) supports IP-in-IP only, NOT GRE.** macOS Sonoma 14.4
  on pico ships gif0 (already present, POINTOPOINT,MULTICAST flag, not
  UP). gre(4) is BSD-specific and not in darwin. So this approach is
  IP-in-IP (proto 4 / IPPROTO_IPV4) end-to-end, not GRE proper. Linux
  side uses `ip tunnel add mode ipip`.
- **Why IP-in-IP solves the asymmetric routing problem TPROXY can't**:
  the tunnel is bidirectional and stateful at the routing layer. Reply
  packets from pico are routed back through gif0 via a /32 route for
  the original client (or, more practically, via an inner-tunnel /30
  with route-rewriting at zig-computer's iptables). Pico does NOT need
  to flip its default route; only specific destinations get the tunnel.
- **Layering on Tailscale**: option A is "carry the tunnel over the
  public IP path direct (proto 4 may be blocked by ISP/firewall on
  one or both ends)"; option B is "carry over tailnet IPs" — meaning
  outer tunnel src/dst are 100.98.174.21 and 100.72.47.4 instead of
  the public IPs. Option B is cleaner because Tailscale's WireGuard
  reliably passes proto 4. **First test uses option B.**
- **No SS14 binary changes needed.** Robust.Server already binds
  to 0.0.0.0; packets arriving at gif0's inner address are
  indistinguishable from packets arriving at any other interface.

## Architecture

Inner tunnel network: `10.99.0.0/30`
- `10.99.0.1` = zig-computer end of tunnel (gre0)
- `10.99.0.2` = pico end of tunnel (gif0)

Outer endpoints (Tailscale-carried for reliability past ISP filters
on protocol 4):
- Outer src zig = 100.98.174.21 (zig-computer tailscale0)
- Outer dst pico = 100.72.47.4 (pico utun5)

Inbound flow (client → game):
1. Client UDP packet hits zig-computer's public IP at :11212
   (src=client-real-IP, dst=51.81.33.136, dport=11212)
2. iptables `mangle PREROUTING` MARKs the packet OR a `DNAT/route`
   redirect rule forwards it into the tunnel without rewriting src.
   The cleanest mechanism is **policy routing**: incoming on ens3
   with dport=11212 → route via gre0 (no NAT). The outer header
   wraps src=tailnet-zig + dst=tailnet-pico; inner header preserves
   original src=client + dst=51.81.33.136.
3. Pico receives outer packet on utun5 (tailnet), kernel decapsulates,
   inner packet appears on gif0. Robust.Server (bound 0.0.0.0:11212)
   accepts it; `RemoteEndPoint.Address` shows client's real IP.

Outbound flow (game → client):
4. Pico's response: src=51.81.33.136 (or 10.99.0.2 — see Open Q's),
   dst=client-real-IP. Routing rule on pico: "for dst=client-real-IP
   (or for src=51.81.33.136), use gif0".
5. Outer wraps src=tailnet-pico + dst=tailnet-zig; arrives at zig-
   computer's gre0.
6. zig-computer iptables `MASQUERADE` or `SNAT` rewrites src to
   51.81.33.136 (if pico responded with 10.99.0.2) and sends out
   ens3 to client.

The asymmetric routing problem dissolves because the tunnel IS the
return path — there is no separate "default route" to mistakenly use.

## Test plan

All tests use port `:11212` (NEVER :1212 which is the live SS14).
Synthetic before real-SS14.

### Step 0 — Snapshot pre-test state

```bash
# zig-computer
sudo iptables-save > /tmp/iptables.pretest.\$(date +%s)
ip rule list > /tmp/iprule.pretest.\$(date +%s)
ip route show table all > /tmp/iproute.pretest.\$(date +%s)
ip tunnel show > /tmp/iptun.pretest.\$(date +%s)
ip addr > /tmp/ipaddr.pretest.\$(date +%s)

# pico
ssh pico 'ifconfig gif0 > /tmp/gif0.pretest 2>&1; netstat -rn > /tmp/route.pretest'
```

### Step 1 — Create IP-in-IP tunnel, zig-computer side (Tailscale-carried)

```bash
# Load ipip module
sudo modprobe ipip

# Create tunnel: outer endpoints = tailnet IPs (proto 4 over WireGuard)
sudo ip tunnel add gre0 mode ipip remote 100.72.47.4 local 100.98.174.21 ttl 64
sudo ip link set gre0 mtu 1400 up
sudo ip addr add 10.99.0.1/30 dev gre0
```

Verify: `ip tunnel show gre0` should list the new tunnel; `ip -4 addr
show gre0` should list 10.99.0.1.

Rollback:
```bash
sudo ip addr del 10.99.0.1/30 dev gre0
sudo ip link set gre0 down
sudo ip tunnel del gre0
# Module stays loaded — harmless
```

### Step 2 — Create gif tunnel, pico side

```bash
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 tunnel 100.72.47.4 100.98.174.21'
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 10.99.0.2 10.99.0.1 netmask 255.255.255.252 up'
ssh pico 'sudo sysctl -w net.inet.ip.forwarding=1'
```

Verify on pico: `ifconfig gif0` shows inet 10.99.0.2 --> 10.99.0.1
and the outer tunnel endpoints. Ping test:
```bash
ping -c 3 10.99.0.2  # from zig-computer
ssh pico 'ping -c 3 10.99.0.1'  # from pico
```

Rollback:
```bash
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 -tunnel'
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 inet 10.99.0.2 delete'
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 down'
ssh pico 'sudo sysctl -w net.inet.ip.forwarding=0'  # IF it was 0 before
```

### Step 3 — Synthetic UDP-echo across the tunnel (NO nginx involved)

This step proves the tunnel works for raw UDP and we can see source
preservation at the tunnel-inner layer. NO production traffic touched.

On pico:
```bash
ssh pico 'nc -u -l 11212' &  # listener bound 0.0.0.0:11212
ssh pico 'sudo tcpdump -ni gif0 port 11212 -c 5' &
```

On zig-computer:
```bash
# Send to pico's tunnel-inner IP (this proves tunnel works at all)
echo "hello-tunnel-\$(date +%s)" | nc -u -q1 10.99.0.2 11212
```

Expected pico tcpdump: `IP 10.99.0.1.<srcport> > 10.99.0.2.11212`
This proves the tunnel carries UDP. Source IP visible at pico = 10.99.0.1
(zig-computer's tunnel-inner). NOT source-preservation yet — that's
the NEXT step.

### Step 4 — Source-preserving forward via policy route (THE actual test)

Now we route INBOUND :11212 traffic from the public interface into
the tunnel WITHOUT rewriting the src IP:

```bash
# Mark inbound :11212 packets
sudo iptables -t mangle -A PREROUTING -i ens3 -p udp --dport 11212 -j MARK --set-mark 0x2

# Policy route marked packets via gre0
sudo ip rule add fwmark 0x2 lookup 200 priority 200
sudo ip route add default dev gre0 table 200

# Allow forwarding for the tunnel (note: sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward
# is likely already 1 due to docker, but verify)
cat /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward  # expect: 1
```

For pico to reply: routes for the public client IP MUST be set, OR
we use a simpler tactic: send replies back through gre0 by virtue
of the inner-route. If we tell pico's gif0 to be the gateway for ALL
non-LAN, non-tailnet traffic from src 10.99.0.2, we'd hijack pico's
internet outbound. Instead, use a strict per-flow route added on
demand for the test src IP:

```bash
# On pico — only for the synthetic test src
ssh pico 'sudo route -n add -host <synthetic-client-IP> -interface gif0'
```

For the synthetic test, the "client IP" is whatever src we spoof from
zig-computer's nc command. Use `nc -s 10.99.0.1` to send from an
already-tunnel-routed address (no need for a special pico route).

Better test: have nginx do the tunnel-injection. Skip iptables MARK
entirely; configure nginx stream listener on :11212 and proxy_pass
to `10.99.0.2:11212` (the pico tunnel-inner IP). Then verify pico
sees src=10.99.0.1 (zig's tunnel-inner) — that's NOT source-preservation,
it's nginx-rewriting. To get source-preservation, add `proxy_bind
\$remote_addr transparent;` AND the iptables mangle + route rule above.

**Decision: split this into two sub-tests.**

#### Step 4a — Tunnel-only (no source preservation, baseline reachability)

nginx stream config (NEW file, port 11212):
```bash
sudo tee /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-gre-test.conf <<'CONF'
# TEST ONLY — port 11212; live SS14 stays on :1212 untouched
upstream gre_test_pico {
    server 10.99.0.2:11212;  # pico's tunnel-inner IP
}
server {
    listen 11212 udp;
    proxy_pass gre_test_pico;
    proxy_responses 1;
    proxy_timeout 10s;
}
CONF
sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx
sudo ufw allow 11212/udp comment 'GRE test'
```

Test from off-host (or from zig-computer's localhost — both show that
the data path works):
```bash
echo "ping-via-tunnel" | nc -u -q1 51.81.33.136 11212
ssh pico 'sudo tcpdump -ni gif0 port 11212 -c 5'
# Expected: src=10.99.0.1, dst=10.99.0.2 (nginx-rewritten as expected)
```

Rollback:
```bash
sudo rm /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-gre-test.conf
sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx
sudo ufw delete allow 11212/udp
```

#### Step 4b — Source-preserving (proxy_bind transparent + tunnel)

This is the END goal. nginx still does the listener, but binds the
forward socket to the client's real src IP. Inside the tunnel, pico
sees src=real-client-IP.

```bash
sudo tee /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-gre-test.conf <<'CONF'
upstream gre_test_pico {
    server 10.99.0.2:11212;
}
server {
    listen 11212 udp;
    proxy_pass gre_test_pico;
    proxy_bind \$remote_addr transparent;
    proxy_responses 1;
    proxy_timeout 10s;
}
CONF
sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx

# Per nginx 1.13.8+, CAP_NET_RAW is auto-inherited by workers. Verify:
getcap /usr/sbin/nginx || true  # may show cap_net_raw, may need to set
```

For the asymmetric return-path:
```bash
# On pico — route the synthetic test src IP back through gif0
# (only the SPECIFIC client IP, NOT default route)
ssh pico 'sudo route -n add -host <test-client-IP> -interface gif0'
```

Test from a known-distinct src IP (best: from metis tailnet client,
OR a vps that's NOT zig-computer):
```bash
# On the test src:
echo "src-preserve-test" | nc -u -q1 51.81.33.136 11212
```

Expected pico tcpdump on gif0: `IP <test-client-IP>.<sport> >
51.81.33.136.11212` — the **real source IP arrives at pico**. PASS.

Rollback (per-client route on pico):
```bash
ssh pico 'sudo route -n delete -host <test-client-IP>'
```

### Step 5 — Real SS14 client test (deferred until 4b passes)

Only run if 4a + 4b cleanly pass AND we've confirmed live SS14 on
:1212 is unaffected:
```bash
ss -tunap | grep :1212  # should show robust.server still bound
```

Real SS14 client test would require:
- A separate Robust.Server instance on pico bound :11212 (NOT touching
  the prod :1212 on zig-computer)
- pico-side route for the connecting player's IP
- Connect SS14 launcher to `51.81.33.136:11212`
- On pico: tcpdump confirms real IP; psql shows real IP if banned

This is STRETCH GOAL — defer to a follow-up bead if 4b passes.

### Step 6 — Teardown (rollback all steps in reverse)

```bash
# Step 4b rollback (per-host routes on pico, nginx config)
ssh pico 'sudo route -n delete -host <test-client-IP>' 2>/dev/null || true
sudo rm -f /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-gre-test.conf
sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx
sudo ufw delete allow 11212/udp 2>/dev/null || true

# Step 4a iptables (if applied)
sudo iptables -t mangle -D PREROUTING -i ens3 -p udp --dport 11212 -j MARK --set-mark 0x2 2>/dev/null || true
sudo ip rule del fwmark 0x2 lookup 200 priority 200 2>/dev/null || true
sudo ip route flush table 200 2>/dev/null || true

# Step 2 pico tunnel
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 -tunnel; sudo ifconfig gif0 inet 10.99.0.2 delete; sudo ifconfig gif0 down'
ssh pico 'sudo sysctl -w net.inet.ip.forwarding=0'  # only if it was 0 before

# Step 1 zig tunnel
sudo ip addr del 10.99.0.1/30 dev gre0 2>/dev/null || true
sudo ip link set gre0 down 2>/dev/null || true
sudo ip tunnel del gre0 2>/dev/null || true
```

Verify rollback:
```bash
diff <(sudo iptables-save) /tmp/iptables.pretest.*  # expect empty
diff <(ip rule list) /tmp/iprule.pretest.*  # expect empty
ip tunnel show  # expect empty
ssh pico 'ifconfig gif0' | grep -v "POINTOPOINT,MULTICAST"  # expect baseline only
sudo nginx -t  # expect "test is successful"
ss -tunap | grep :1212  # expect live SS14 still bound
```

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Step-by-step test plan documented (this section)
- [ ] Each step has explicit rollback documented (above)
- [ ] Step 3 (raw tunnel UDP echo) result: PASS / FAIL recorded
- [ ] Step 4a (nginx-via-tunnel, no source preservation) result: PASS / FAIL
- [ ] Step 4b (nginx-via-tunnel + proxy_bind transparent) result:
      pico tcpdump shows real client IP (PASS) or doesn't (FAIL with diagnosis)
- [ ] Real-SS14-client test result (DEFERRED stretch goal)
- [ ] Rollback verified clean against pre-test snapshots
- [ ] Outcome reflected back to parent 52c bead's --notes

## Open questions

- Does the per-client route on pico scale? (For production: every
  connected player's public IP needs a route. A SS14 server with 30
  players = 30 routes added/removed dynamically. Possibly script via
  pf table + a connection-tracking hook. Out of scope for first test.)
- Is there a simpler pf rdr rule on pico that obviates per-client
  routes? (`pass in on gif0 reply-to (utun5 ...)` style — needs
  research.)
- Does Tailscale block protocol-4 inside the WireGuard tunnel? (Likely
  not — WireGuard carries arbitrary IP payloads — but verify with a
  raw `ping -F 4` or by checking tcpdump on utun5.)
- MTU: gif default 1280, tailscale MTU 1280. IPIP adds 20 bytes header.
  Inside-tailnet payload would be 1260. SS14's UDP packets are well
  under 1KB — should be fine. Verify with `ping -s 1200 -M do`.
notes:
## Phase 3 — execution attempt (2026-05-24)

Executed the test plan steps; got to Step 2 (tunnel up on both ends) then
hit a fundamental blocker that aborts the chosen approach. Detail:

### Step 0 — Pre-test snapshot — DONE
- zig-computer: iptables-save (187 lines), ip rule (7), ip route (39),
  ip tunnel (empty), ip addr — all saved to /tmp/*.pretest.1779647689
- pico: ifconfig gif0 (POINTOPOINT,MULTICAST, no inet), netstat -rn,
  net.inet.ip.forwarding=0 — all saved to /tmp/*.pretest.* on pico
- Confirmed live SS14 on :1212: 4 listeners (UDP v4+v6, TCP v4+v6)

### Step 1 — Linux ipip tunnel — DONE
```
sudo modprobe ipip
sudo ip tunnel add gre0 mode ipip remote 100.72.47.4 local 100.98.174.21 ttl 64
sudo ip link set gre0 mtu 1400 up
sudo ip addr add 10.99.0.1/30 dev gre0
```
Result: gre0@NONE UP, link/ipip 100.98.174.21 peer 100.72.47.4,
inet 10.99.0.1/30. Clean.

### Step 2 — macOS gif tunnel — DONE
```
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 tunnel 100.72.47.4 100.98.174.21'
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 10.99.0.2 10.99.0.1 netmask 255.255.255.252 up'
ssh pico 'sudo sysctl -w net.inet.ip.forwarding=1'
```
Result: `gif0: flags=8051<UP,POINTOPOINT,RUNNING,MULTICAST> mtu 1280
	tunnel inet 100.72.47.4 --> 100.98.174.21
	inet 10.99.0.2 --> 10.99.0.1 netmask 0xfffffffc`. Clean.

### Step 2.5 — Connectivity verification — FAIL (DIVERGENCE)

```
ping -c 3 -W 2 10.99.0.2   # from zig-computer
```
Result: **100% packet loss**. tcpdump on pico's utun5 (the tailscale
interface) shows pico IS emitting proto-4 packets:
```
IP 100.72.47.4 > 100.98.174.21: IP 10.99.0.2 > 10.99.0.1: ICMP echo request
```
But tcpdump on zig-computer's tailscale0 shows ONLY TCP/SSH traffic;
**zero proto-4 packets ever arrive at zig-computer**.

### Root cause — Tailscale drops non-TCP/UDP/ICMP IP protocols

The test plan's Open Question #3 ("Does Tailscale block protocol-4
inside the WireGuard tunnel?") is answered: YES, it does. Confirmed by
upstream tailscale issue #14006 "IPIP is not working over tailscale0"
(open since 2024-11-02, unresolved). Tailscale's wireguard
implementation does not relay arbitrary IP protocols; it restricts to
the protocols its userspace stack handles (TCP, UDP, ICMP).

### What this means for the approach

Option A — "carry the tunnel inside Tailscale (outer src/dst = tailnet
IPs)": **BLOCKED by tailscale**. Cannot proceed without an upstream
tailscale fix or a userspace workaround.

Option B — "carry the tunnel over the public IPs (outer src=zig-
computer public, outer dst=pico's home WAN public)": **violates the
core constraint** of this exploration. Pico's home WAN IP would
become the destination of the encapsulated packets — anyone running
tcpdump on the path between zig-computer and pico's home would see
the home WAN IP. Same exposure problem as the rejected DNAT plan.

Option C — "carry the tunnel over a different cloud-relay" (e.g., a
second VPS in the same DC as zig-computer that relays to pico's home
via a non-Tailscale link): defeats the purpose. We already have a
non-Tailscale link (the public internet); the entire point of routing
via zig-computer is that zig-computer's IP is the only one visible.

### Outcome — STOP per "if a step diverges, STOP and report"

Reached the divergence at the connectivity test (Step 2.5, before any
synthetic UDP-echo at Step 3). The architectural premise of "use the
existing tailnet as the tunnel carrier" doesn't hold; the alternatives
either re-introduce the home-IP-exposure problem (option B) or are
escalations beyond the scope of "test in an afternoon" (option C).

### Rollback — DONE and VERIFIED

```
# zig-computer
sudo ip addr del 10.99.0.1/30 dev gre0
sudo ip link set gre0 down
sudo ip tunnel del gre0

# pico
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 -tunnel; sudo ifconfig gif0 inet 10.99.0.2 delete; sudo ifconfig gif0 down'
ssh pico 'sudo sysctl -w net.inet.ip.forwarding=0'
```

Verification against pre-test snapshots:
- iptables: diff shows ONLY packet counter changes (expected); no rule changes
- ip rule: empty diff (clean)
- ip route: empty diff (clean)
- ip tunnel: only tunl0 (kernel default, harmless; appeared via modprobe ipip)
- nginx: config test clean (we never touched nginx in this run — short-
  circuited before Step 4)
- pico gif0: tunnel cleared, no inet addrs; flags show
  `POINTOPOINT,RUNNING,MULTICAST` vs baseline `POINTOPOINT,MULTICAST`
  (RUNNING flag is a one-way once-up indicator in BSD; tunnel config is
  fully torn down — confirmed by absence of `tunnel inet ...` lines)
- pico net.inet.ip.forwarding: back to 0 (matches baseline)
- SS14 :1212 live listeners: 4 throughout (no production impact)

### Verdict

**Synthetic UDP-echo: NOT_RUN** (blocked at tunnel-connectivity step)
**Real SS14 client: NOT_RUN**
**Rollback to pre-test: YES, verified**

### Status flip

This bead's PASS/FAIL question is answered "FAIL for the
Tailscale-carried variant; UNTESTED for the bare-public variant
because the bare-public variant requires the very thing we're avoiding
(exposing pico's home WAN IP)". 

Closing this approach as not viable WITHOUT additional infra
investment (e.g., a second cloud relay) OR an upstream Tailscale fix
to issue #14006. Marking the bead as ready for orchestrator review.

Dependencies:
  -> dotfiles-52c (parent-child) - explore: SS14-on-pico via TPROXY or GRE — preserve source IPs + VPS-IP public face

## CLOSED 2026-05-24 — tested, BLOCKED by tailscale#14006

GRE/IPIP-over-Tailscale tested end-to-end. Tunnel comes up clean on
both ends; ICMP/UDP across the tunnel = 100% loss. tcpdump confirms
pico emits proto-4 packets on utun5; zig-computer's tailscale0 sees
zero. Root cause: Tailscale's WireGuard implementation drops non-TCP/
UDP/ICMP IP protocols (upstream tailscale/tailscale#14006, open since
2024-11-02, unresolved as of writing).

Production safety: zero impact on live SS14. All rollback verified.
ipip kernel module loaded as a passive side effect (sink dev tunl0
appeared, harmless).

A non-Tailscale tunnel endpoint (Pi-on-LAN) was considered but doesn't
solve the underlying goal — the Pi→pico hop has the same asymmetric-
routing problem as TPROXY-to-macOS, with no clean fix without making
the Pi pico's default gateway (hurts all outbound).

**Superseded by**: dotfiles-52c continues; the C2 spec bead (opening
next) becomes the live work track. jjd stays closed as a substantive
test result, not a deferral.

Dependencies:
  -> dotfiles-52c (parent-child) - explore: SS14-on-pico via TPROXY or GRE — preserve source IPs + VPS-IP public face

## Closed 2026-05-25 — not viable
GRE/IP-in-IP over Tailscale rejected via tailscale#14006 (Tailscale's WireGuard transport doesn't carry non-TCP/UDP/ICMP). Documented in dotfiles-52c --notes. The C2 wrapper architecture (dotfiles-9g1) was the eventual answer, technically shipped + rolled back per dotfiles-v1i.

title: nightly-digest: failure on 2026-05-26 — see /home/ubuntu/.local/share/claude-digests/logs/2026-05-26.log
a1111: upcast_attn insufficient for SDXL inpaint on MPS — escalate to --no-half
description:
## Context

Follow-up to dotfiles-43l (closed earlier today). 43l added
`upcast_attn: true` to config.json — the Settings-only toggle that the
NaN error message itself recommended. The fix was applied as documented
(file installed, LaunchAgent kickstarted, A1111 booted clean), but user
re-tested inpaint immediately after and the NaN still fires:

```
NansException: A tensor with NaNs was produced in Unet. ... Try setting
the "Upcast cross attention layer to float32" option in Settings >
Stable Diffusion or using the --no-half commandline argument to fix this.
```

Verified `~/stable-diffusion-webui/config.json` contains
`{"upcast_attn": true}` and the file is at the correct location. The
setting key matches A1111 source (`shared_options.py:182`). Despite all
that, the NaN check still trips on inpainting attempts. Likely cause:
on MPS, SDXL inpainting has additional fp16 underflow paths beyond the
cross-attention layer that the upcast doesn't cover.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. A1111 on pico per rp0 + 43l (config.json has `upcast_attn: true`)
2. Load SDXL base 1.0
3. Open Img2Img → Inpaint
4. Upload image, draw mask, generate (any prompt, default settings)
5. After ~14s, NansException fires

Repeatable: user has seen it on multiple consecutive inpaint attempts
since 43l's fix.

## Fix

Escalate from "upcast just the cross-attention layer" to "--no-half"
(full fp32 throughout the model). The error message lists this as the
alternative recommended remedy. Trade-off: SDXL memory footprint
doubles from ~6.5GB to ~13GB — fine on M1 Max with 64GB RAM.

Changes to `~/dotfiles/a1111/webui-user.sh`:
- Drop `--upcast-sampling` ("no effect with --no-half" per A1111's
  own help text)
- Add `--no-half`
- Keep `--no-half-vae` (was already there; redundant under --no-half
  but harmless)
- Keep `upcast_attn: true` in config.json as belt-and-suspenders;
  technically moot but doesn't hurt

## Regression guard

- Structural: webui-user.sh in dotfiles carries --no-half as part of
  COMMANDLINE_ARGS. Fresh-Mac rebuild (mac.setup.sh) installs the
  current webui-user.sh, so the fix is durable.
- Documentation: long-form comment in webui-user.sh explains WHY
  --no-half over --upcast-sampling (because the lighter path tested
  insufficient for SDXL inpaint on MPS in this real-world test).

## Acceptance criteria

- [x] webui-user.sh updated with --no-half
- [x] Pico's live webui-user.sh synced
- [x] LaunchAgent kickstarted, A1111 boots clean with new flag
- [ ] User re-tests inpaint, confirms NaN no longer fires
- [ ] Performance impact (memory + iteration speed) acceptable
notes:
## CLOSED 2026-05-24 — verified by user

User retested SDXL inpaint after --no-half landed; inpaint works, no
NaN. Performance impact deemed acceptable in the field (no complaint
on speed/memory; M1 Max 64GB carries the doubled SDXL footprint
without strain).

title: watchdog: strip HermesGateway probe (was the only kanban-touching component; Phase C-lite)
description:
## Steps to Reproduce
1. Watchdog ticks every 5 min via systemd-user timer
2. HermesGatewayJob.probe() opens a fresh SQLite connection to ~/.hermes/kanban.db via 'hermes kanban diagnostics --json' + 'hermes kanban show <tid>'
3. Revive may invoke 'hermes kanban block <tid> <reason>' — a SQLite WRITE from outside the gateway
4. Under load, the gateway's own dispatcher writes simultaneously → WAL multi-writer contention
5. Result: kanban.db corruption (verified 2026-05-28T04:53Z, six .corrupt.bak files in 30 seconds; recovery via fresh hermes kanban init lost all task history)
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] HermesGatewayJob removed from zig-watchdog.py + tests
 - [ ] pytest passes; ruff clean
 - [ ] No 'HermesGateway|HERMES_BIN|HERMES_CRASH|hermes_kanban_|hermes_dispatch|hermes_diagnostics' references remain
 - [ ] Watchdog touches: filesystem, dashboard HTTP, pico SSH only. No Hermes contact.

title: hooks: harden pre-bead-close.sh — worktree guard + scrutiny gate
description:
## Context
"Subagents never run br close / br update --status; bead lifecycle is
orchestrator-only" is repeated across AGENTS.md, /beads, /orchestrator,
/handoff — repeated that often because it gets broken. A subagent
finishing work has the bead ID and br on PATH; the .beads/ symlink
makes a stray close mutate the orchestrator's shared state immediately.
Separately, /scrutinize says an -t impl bead closes only on a SHIP
verdict, but nothing enforces it.

## Task
Add two guards to pre-bead-close.sh (PreToolUse:Bash):
1. Worktree guard — when cwd is inside .claude/worktrees/agent-*, block
   `br close` and `br update --status` (still allow `br update --notes`,
   which /beads explicitly permits subagents to do).
2. Scrutiny gate — closing a `-t impl` bead is blocked unless its
   br-show output carries a SHIP (or OVERRIDE) scrutiny verdict.
Preserve the existing lint gate and chained-cd handling.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Worktree cwd blocks br close + br update --status; allows br update --notes
- [ ] Non-worktree cwd unaffected (orchestrator runs lifecycle normally)
- [ ] -t impl bead with no SHIP/OVERRIDE verdict blocked; with one, allowed
- [ ] Existing lint gate + chained-cd behaviour preserved
- [ ] test-pre-bead-close.sh extended and green
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] worktree guard: blocks br close + br update --status, allows br update --notes
 - [ ] scrutiny gate: -t impl beads need a SHIP/OVERRIDE verdict to close
 - [ ] existing lint + chained-cd behaviour preserved; test green

title: fix: autonovel-phase1 cron broken — runner.py path stale after Wave 18 SSoT cleanup
description:
## Problem
Daily autonovel Phase 1 cron (`f63079296927`, runs 09:00 UTC) has been failing since Wave 18:

    Last run:  2026-05-28T09:00:22.509161+00:00  error: Script exited with code 2
    stderr: python3: can't open file '/home/ubuntu/.hermes/skills/autonovel-phase1-smoke/runner.py': [Errno 2] No such file or directory

## Symptom
Phase 1 (the supposedly-working autonovel pipeline per handoff) hasn't actually run since at least the Wave 18 SSoT cleanup. Each invocation exits 2 immediately.

## Root cause (suspected)
Wave 18 deleted duplicate profile-local skill dirs from `~/.hermes/skills/` to fix the Hermes `--skills` ambiguous-paths resolver bug. The autonovel-phase1-smoke SKILL was canonized into `external_dirs` (lives at `~/explore/autonovel/hermes-skills/autonovel-phase1-smoke/runner.py`).

But the `run-autonovel.sh` cron script hardcodes the absolute path `python3 /home/ubuntu/.hermes/skills/autonovel-phase1-smoke/runner.py`. The cleanup removed the file at that path; the script can no longer find it.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Phase 1 cron fires at 09:00 UTC daily
2. Script tries to invoke `python3 ~/.hermes/skills/autonovel-phase1-smoke/runner.py`
3. File doesn't exist (it lives at `~/explore/autonovel/hermes-skills/autonovel-phase1-smoke/runner.py`)
4. Exit 2, no novel produced

## Fix
Update `~/explore/autonovel/cron/run-autonovel.sh` (or wherever the runner-path is hardcoded) to point at `~/explore/autonovel/hermes-skills/autonovel-phase1-smoke/runner.py` directly. SSoT model: external_dirs is canonical.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] run-autonovel.sh references the external_dirs path, not the deleted `~/.hermes/skills/` path
- [ ] Manual test fires the cron via `hermes cron run f63079296927` and exits 0
- [ ] Regression: a follow-up Wave-18-style cleanup wouldn't break it (use a $HERMES_SKILLS_DIR env or symlink discipline)
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] run-autonovel.sh references external_dirs path
 - [ ] hermes cron run f63079296927 exits 0
 - [ ] Regression: no hardcoded ~/.hermes/skills/ paths in cron scripts
notes: SUPERSEDED 2026-05-28 by autonovel:bd-ju5 — moved to correct project tree per bead-location-discipline. autonovel work belongs in ~/explore/autonovel/.beads/, not dotfiles.

title: fix: zig-watchdog probes can race kanban dispatcher writes (SQLite corruption risk)
description:
## Problem

Kanban DB at /home/ubuntu/.hermes/kanban.db corrupted ~04:53 UTC during this
session ("sqlite refused to open file: database disk image is malformed").
Hermes auto-created 6+ .corrupt.bak files in 30 seconds (same image, sequential
timestamps — the corruption was already present at first backup). `.recover`
yielded schema only (0 INSERT statements) — data unrecoverable. All task
history lost: t_360fa078, t_2d3dcc3e (weekly-reporting), t_093c0add, etc.

## Symptom

`hermes kanban list` (or any kanban CLI) → "Refusing to open corrupt kanban
DB". Gateway log: "kanban dispatcher: board default database ... is not a
valid SQLite database; disabling dispatch for this board". User-facing impact:
Phase 2 substrate down; cross-arc kanban workloads stop dispatching.

## Likely cause

Concurrent writers on a single SQLite file with insufficient coordination:
- gateway-embedded dispatcher tick-loop (every 60s)
- watchdog systemd-timer tick (every 5min), which calls
  `hermes kanban dispatch --dry-run`, `hermes kanban diagnostics --json`,
  `hermes kanban show <tid>` (added in bd-cke fix) per actionable crash-loop
- ad-hoc CLI calls (this session: dispatch / show / block / unblock during
  debugging)
- worker subprocess spawn-crash cycles re-claiming + writing event rows

SQLite WAL mode handles concurrent readers + 1 writer, but multiple
fork/exec processes coordinating via WAL/SHM mmap with stale snapshot reads
can desync. The dispatcher's stuck-FD pattern documented earlier this
session was a symptom of similar pressure.

## Steps to Reproduce

Not deterministically reproducible yet. Best evidence:
1. Long-running hermes gateway (3+ hours)
2. Run zig-watchdog systemd timer firing every 5min on the same kanban DB
3. Add ad-hoc kanban CLI calls during a worker crash-loop scenario
4. After ~30min of mixed activity: `database disk image is malformed`

## Fix (proposed)

Three layers of defense:

### F1 — Watchdog write-coordination
Either (a) skip kanban probes when a dispatcher tick is mid-flight (filelock
on ~/.hermes/.dispatcher-tick.lock), OR (b) increase watchdog interval to
10min (less frequent racing), OR (c) make watchdog probes read-only via
sqlite3 directly with `?mode=ro&immutable=1` URI rather than spawning hermes
CLI subprocesses that may open WAL writers.

### F2 — Backup discipline
The corruption was already present in the FIRST .corrupt.bak. Hermes should
maintain a rolling daily-good snapshot (`kanban.db.daily.bak`) created via
`sqlite3 source ".backup target"` (atomic) rather than just preserving the
corrupt file. The watchdog could own this.

### F3 — Don't issue manual CLI calls while dispatcher is hot
Operational discipline: when investigating, query state via sqlite3 in
read-only mode rather than spawning more hermes CLI processes that contend.

## Source of Report

Empirical observation 2026-05-28 ~04:53 UTC during the dotfiles-cke fix
verification: t_360fa078 worker spawned (PID-tracked), watchdog probe ran a
new kanban-show call, gateway dispatcher continued its 60s tick — within
2 minutes the DB was malformed.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] F1 implemented (one of a/b/c) with test
- [ ] F2 implemented + scheduled (daily good-state snapshot)
- [ ] F3 documented in ~/dotfiles/local-models/GUARDRAILS.md as a new G-entry
- [ ] Reproduction recipe captured (or marked as "could not reproduce")
- [ ] Bead: dotfiles-ns3 in commit trailer
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] F1 implemented (one of a/b/c) with test coverage
 - [ ] F2 implemented + scheduled (daily good-state snapshot)
 - [ ] F3 documented in GUARDRAILS.md as new G-entry
 - [ ] Reproduction recipe captured (or marked as unreproducible)
 - [ ] Bead: dotfiles-ns3 in commit trailer
notes: SUPERSEDED 2026-05-30 by dotfiles-lzs (Phase C-lite). The race-condition this bead described required an outside-the-gateway writer (zig-watchdog's HermesGateway probe). Phase C-lite stripped that probe entirely; watchdog no longer touches kanban.db. No outside writer = no race. F1/F2/F3 defenses no longer needed.

title: spec: zig-watchdog — mechanical stall detection + revive for long-running jobs (bench, model pulls, Hermes gateway)
description:
# Spec: zig-watchdog — mechanical stall/crash detection + revive for long-running jobs

## 1. Overview

Single Python script + systemd-user timer that periodically probes a registered set
of long-running job classes for stall/crash patterns and either revives them
mechanically or alerts the operator. Replaces the current "user nudges Claude when
something dies" pattern with mechanical discipline that runs autonomously and
survives Claude session boundaries.

**This skill is structurally analogous to the autonovel-phase1 substrate**: a
deterministic-script cron-equivalent (systemd timer) running every N minutes, no
LLM in the path, with structured probe()/revive() per concern.

## 2. Baseline / current state

Two failures THIS SESSION proved the need:

1. **Kimi-Linear pull stalled for 8h** in CLOSE_WAIT socket state with no progress;
   user had to nudge ("make sure the kimi download isnt hanging") to surface it.
   Recovery was mechanical: kill + restart hf download.
2. **bench-matrix.sh aborted** at cell 3/9 when Ollama on pico became wedged in a
   wedged-generation state; user had to nudge ("dont forget my SPA request" implied
   they noticed I'd missed the matrix dying). Recovery was mechanical: pkill ollama,
   restart, bench-matrix --resume.

Plus a third found AFTER asking for the watchdog spec:

3. **autonovel kanban worker crash-looped 99 times** in ~96 min, each ~60s. Dispatcher
   kept respawning despite failure_limit=2 because of gave_up→promoted cycling.
   Mechanical fix: detect ≥3 crashes within 5 min on same task → block the task.

All three share an exact shape: long-running process, PID alive (or pseudo-alive),
observably stalled (file mtime stale OR endpoint hung OR rapid spawn-crash cycle).

## 3. Changes / decisions

- **Architecture**: Single Python 3 script `~/.local/share/zig-watchdog/watchdog.py`,
  stdlib only. Plugin pattern — each long-job class implements `Probe` (returns OK |
  STALL | CRASH | UNKNOWN) and `Revive` (idempotent fix). New job classes = add a
  Python class, no infra change.
- **Trigger**: systemd-user timer `zig-watchdog.timer`, OnUnitActiveSec=5min,
  Persistent=true (survives reboot).
- **Service unit**: `zig-watchdog.service` Type=oneshot, ExecStart=python3 watchdog.py
- **Logs**: `/tmp/zig-watchdog.log` (rotated by systemd journal anyway), plus per-tick
  results stashed to `~/.cache/zig-watchdog/state.json` for the SPA to consume.
- **Notification**: writes alerts to `~/.cache/zig-watchdog/alerts.json` (append).
  The bench-dashboard SPA (already shipped at port 8765) reads this and surfaces
  via the browser Notifications API (user-confirmed flavor). Pure tailnet.
- **Self-monitoring exclusion**: watchdog does NOT depend on Hermes (avoids the
  circular dependency caught earlier this session when the gateway disk-I/O
  appeared blocked).

## 4. Formal specification

### 4.1 Job classes (v1)

| Class | Probe | Revive |
|---|---|---|
| `BenchMatrix` | (a) pgrep bench-matrix.sh; (b) check newest results/HEALTHCHECK_FAIL-*.txt mtime; (c) curl pico Ollama+MLX endpoints | If aborted-by-healthcheck AND backends recovered: `nohup bench-matrix.sh --smoke --resume > /tmp/.../log &`. Cooldown: 10min between restart attempts. |
| `HfDownload` | For each `hf download` PID on pico: ssh check CPU=0 AND `.incomplete` file mtime older than 5min | kill -9 PID; restart with same args from log file; preserve partial downloads (HF resumes file-level) |
| `HermesGateway` | (a) systemctl --user is-active hermes-gateway; (b) check `hermes kanban dispatch --dry-run --json` succeeds; (c) check for repeated crash patterns in last 5min — task with ≥3 crashes in 5min should auto-block | (a) restart service if inactive; (b) `hermes kanban block <task_id>` if crash-loop detected |
| `BenchDashboard` | curl http://localhost:8765/healthz | restart via start-dashboard.sh |

### 4.2 Plugin interface

```python
class JobClass:
    name: str
    probe_interval_seconds: int = 300  # default 5min
    cooldown_seconds: int = 600         # 10min between revive attempts per job
    max_revive_attempts: int = 3        # before alerting

    def probe(self) -> ProbeResult: ...
    def revive(self) -> ReviveResult: ...
```

`ProbeResult = OK | STALL | CRASH | UNKNOWN` (string enum)
`ReviveResult = SUCCESS | FAILED` (string enum)

### 4.3 State machine per job

```
NORMAL ── probe=STALL/CRASH ──► REVIVING ── revive=SUCCESS ──► NORMAL
                                  │
                                  └─ revive=FAILED ──► (incr counter) ──► if counter≥3: ALERTING
```

### 4.4 Output schema (`~/.cache/zig-watchdog/state.json`)

```json
{
  "last_tick_utc": "2026-05-28T03:00:00Z",
  "jobs": {
    "BenchMatrix": {
      "last_probe": "OK",
      "last_probe_utc": "...",
      "last_revive_attempt_utc": null,
      "revive_attempts": 0,
      "alerting": false
    },
    "HfDownload": {...},
    ...
  }
}
```

### 4.5 Alerts schema (`~/.cache/zig-watchdog/alerts.json`)

Append-only JSON-lines for the SPA to read:
```json
{"utc": "2026-05-28T03:05:00Z", "job": "HermesGateway", "kind": "crash-loop-detected", "detail": "task t_360fa078 crashed 5x in 5min", "auto_action": "blocked"}
```

### 4.6 SPA integration (existing bench-dashboard)

- Add `GET /api/alerts.json` endpoint returning the tail-of alerts.json
- Add JS that polls /api/alerts.json every 5s and fires `new Notification(...)` on new entries (after `Notification.requestPermission()`)
- Add a red banner on the dashboard when any job is `alerting=true`

## 5. Test cases (≥10)

TEST: probe_bench_matrix_ok_when_healthcheck_recent
TEST: probe_bench_matrix_stall_when_aborted_and_backends_healthy
TEST: probe_hf_download_stall_when_cpu_zero_and_mtime_old
TEST: probe_hf_download_ok_when_file_growing
TEST: probe_hermes_crash_loop_when_3_crashes_in_5min
TEST: probe_hermes_ok_when_dispatch_dry_run_succeeds
TEST: revive_bench_matrix_runs_command_and_logs
TEST: revive_hf_download_kills_and_restarts
TEST: revive_hermes_blocks_crash_loop_task
TEST: max_revive_attempts_triggers_alert
TEST: cooldown_prevents_immediate_re_revive
TEST: state_json_written_each_tick

## 6. Open questions

- OQ-01 (P1): Run on zig only, or also on pico for HfDownload-on-pico? **DECIDED: zig only**, but `HfDownload.probe()` uses `ssh pico` to inspect remote processes. Less infra.
- OQ-02 (P1): Watchdog timer interval? **DECIDED: 5min** (matches typical stall-detection windows).
- OQ-03 (P2): Alert delivery? **DECIDED: browser Notification API via SPA** (user-confirmed). ntfy.sh self-hosted is v2.
- OQ-04 (P3): Self-monitoring (watchdog itself dies)? **DEFERRED — systemd Restart=on-failure on timer; manual `journalctl --user -u zig-watchdog -f` for explicit health.

## 7. Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Single file at `~/dotfiles/local-models/zig-watchdog.py` (stdlib only)
- [ ] systemd-user timer + service at `~/dotfiles/local-models/zig-watchdog.{service,timer}` + installer script
- [ ] ≥12 pytest tests (per §5) all pass
- [ ] First mechanical action: detects today's blocked t_360fa078 + bench-matrix abort + queues smoke restart
- [ ] SPA adds /api/alerts.json + browser Notification + red banner widget
- [ ] Bead: dotfiles-olh in commit trailer

## NOT in scope (v1)

- ntfy.sh self-hosted (v2)
- Push to user's phone outside Notification API
- Watchdog-watchdog (the self-monitor)
- Cross-host probing beyond pico (just one remote)
- Job-class plugin loading from external file (v1 has them in-source)
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Single file at local-models/zig-watchdog.py (stdlib only)
 - [ ] systemd-user timer + service + installer script
 - [ ] ≥12 pytest tests all pass
 - [ ] Mechanical action: blocks crash-loops, restarts stalled hf-downloads, restarts aborted bench-matrix
 - [ ] SPA gets /api/alerts.json + browser Notification + red banner widget
 - [ ] Bead: dotfiles-olh in commit trailer

title: ss14-c2-cutover: 11-step playbook — flip SS14 from zig-computer to pico via wrapper
description:
## Context

Final live-cutover bead for Approach C2 (spec dotfiles-9g1). All upstream work has shipped:

- **dotfiles**: ss14-wrapper Go daemon (impl 1bi SHIP, xx9 integration test PASS, b9o deploy artifacts ready)
- **vacation-station-14**: C# patches (vs-4h1 + vs-q7m fix wave SHIP); azigler/RobustToolbox fork live; .gitmodules updated
- **pico**: ss14-wrapper LaunchAgent loaded, listening on 100.72.47.4:11214 (UDP+TCP), HEALTH probe OK; ARM64 SS14 + watchdog build artifacts already on pico from Phase 4 attempt (preserved)
- **zig-computer**: SS14 currently LIVE serving real players; nginx ready for `stream{}` block

This bead drives the actual flip. Production-affecting (drops players currently connected); needs an off-hours window + the user's hand on the wheel.

## Pre-flight checklist

Before running ANY step, confirm:

- [ ] Current player count on `ss14.zig.computer` is 0 (or low + announced maintenance)
- [ ] pico is healthy: `ssh pico 'echo HEALTH | nc -U /tmp/ss14-wrapper.sock'` returns `OK active_sessions=N uptime_s=M`
- [ ] vs14 is up-to-date on pico: `ssh pico 'cd ~/vacation-station-14 && git pull && git submodule update --init --recursive'` — should pull azigler/RobustToolbox + the C# patches
- [ ] SS14 build on pico is current: `ssh pico 'cd ~/vacation-station-14 && ./BuildContent.py'` (or equivalent) — verify build succeeds
- [ ] Snapshot zig-computer nginx config: `sudo cp /etc/nginx/sites-available/ss14.zig.computer.conf /tmp/ss14-nginx.bak.$(date +%s)`
- [ ] Snapshot pico Robust.Server config: `ssh pico 'cp /opt/ss14-watchdog/instances/vacation-station/config.toml /tmp/vs14-config.bak.$(date +%s)'`
- [ ] ban_address row count snapshot: `psql -h pico -U vs14 -d vacation_station -c "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ban_address;"` — record number to compare post-cutover
- [ ] Maintenance window announced (Discord / hub status / wherever)
- [ ] Rollback runbook reviewed: every step below has its rollback documented inline

## The 11-step playbook (per spec dotfiles-9g1 §4.6)

### Step 1 — On pico: configure wrapper-aware cvars in pico's Robust.Server config

Add to `/opt/ss14-watchdog/instances/vacation-station/config.toml`:

```toml
[net]
use_wrapper_remote_address = true
wrapper_socket = "/tmp/ss14-wrapper.sock"
```

Plus the existing `[status]` config — `bind = "127.0.0.1:1213"` (NOT `*:1212`; bind loopback so only wrapper reaches it).

**Rollback**: revert to snapshot `/tmp/vs14-config.bak.*`.

### Step 2 — On pico: update wrapper LaunchAgent upstream

Current wrapper's `SS14_WRAPPER_UPSTREAM=127.0.0.1:11215` (dev/test default). Flip to `127.0.0.1:1213` (where Robust.Server will bind):

```bash
ssh pico 'sed -i.bak "s|127.0.0.1:11215|127.0.0.1:1213|" ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ss14-wrapper.plist'
ssh pico 'launchctl kickstart -k gui/$(id -u pico)/com.zig.ss14-wrapper'
```

Verify wrapper restarted clean: `ssh pico 'echo HEALTH | nc -U /tmp/ss14-wrapper.sock'`.

**Rollback**: revert plist `.bak`, kickstart again.

### Step 3 — On pico: load SS14 watchdog LaunchAgents

The watchdog plist at `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ss14-watchdog.plist` is on disk but unloaded (per Phase 4 rollback). Load it:

```bash
ssh pico 'launchctl load -w ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ss14-watchdog.plist'
ssh pico 'launchctl load -w ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ss14-replay-rotate.plist'
```

Watchdog will start Robust.Server (binding to 127.0.0.1:1213). Verify:

```bash
ssh pico 'lsof -iTCP:1213 -sTCP:LISTEN; lsof -iUDP:1213'
ssh pico 'curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:1213/status | head -3'
```

**Rollback**: `launchctl unload -w ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ss14-watchdog.plist` + same for replay-rotate.

### Step 4 — Smoke test: synthetic UDP from another tailnet device → wrapper → Robust.Server

From zig-computer:

```bash
python3 -c "
import socket
header = b'PROXY TCP4 192.0.2.99 100.72.47.4 64000 11214\r\n'
sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
sock.bind(('', 56000))
# Send a Lidgren ping (well-formed empty payload won't actually be parsed,
# but it'll register a session in the wrapper)
sock.sendto(header + b'\\x00\\x00\\x00\\x00', ('100.72.47.4', 11214))
"
sleep 1
ssh pico 'echo "ENUMERATE" | nc -U /tmp/ss14-wrapper.sock'
```

Should see `udp <fanout-port> 192.0.2.99:64000` in the ENUMERATE output. Confirms wrapper sees synthetic client + tracks real IP.

**Rollback**: nothing to roll back — synthetic test only.

### Step 5 — On zig-computer: write the nginx stream{} block (PROD)

`/etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-game.conf`:

```nginx
upstream ss14_pico_udp {
    server 100.72.47.4:11214;
}
upstream ss14_pico_tcp {
    server 100.72.47.4:11214;
}
server {
    listen 1212 udp;
    proxy_pass ss14_pico_udp;
    proxy_protocol on;
    proxy_timeout 120s;
    proxy_responses 1;
}
server {
    listen 1212;
    proxy_pass ss14_pico_tcp;
    proxy_protocol on;
    proxy_timeout 120s;
}
```

(Exact form per OQ-01 decision: 120s proxy_timeout grounded in Lidgren PingInterval=1s default per /check dotfiles-9cj.)

**Rollback**: `sudo rm /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-game.conf && sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx`.

### Step 6 — On zig-computer: nginx -t + reload

```bash
sudo nginx -t
sudo systemctl reload nginx
```

If `-t` fails, abort and fix the conf before reload.

**Rollback**: same as Step 5.

### Step 7 — On zig-computer: stop ss14-watchdog + replay-rotate (the LIVE services)

```bash
sudo systemctl stop ss14-watchdog
sudo systemctl disable ss14-watchdog
sudo systemctl stop ss14-replay-rotate.timer
sudo systemctl disable ss14-replay-rotate.timer
```

At this point, SS14 is REACHABLE via nginx → pico's wrapper → pico's Robust.Server. Players currently connected to zig-computer drop. Reconnect lands on pico.

**Rollback**: `sudo systemctl enable --now ss14-watchdog ss14-replay-rotate.timer`. SS14 is back on zig within ~30s.

### Step 8 — End-to-end client connection test

Open SS14 launcher → connect to `ss14://ss14.zig.computer` → expect to land in lobby → join round → play for 60s. Confirm:

- Connection succeeds
- Player count in `/status` increments correctly
- No connection-drop / disconnect events in `/tmp/ss14-wrapper.log` on pico

**Rollback**: if connection fails, roll back Steps 7 → 5 (re-enable zig watchdog, remove nginx stream block).

### Step 9 — Ban-address real-IP verification (the architectural goal)

Issue a test ban from an admin client (in-game command), then check the DB:

```bash
psql -h pico -U vs14 -d vacation_station -c "SELECT address FROM ban_address ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 5;"
```

Most-recent row's address should be the **real client IP** (NOT 100.72.47.4 / nginx's IP). Confirms IRemoteAddressOverride is functioning end-to-end through the SS14 moderation path.

**Rollback**: roll back if the address is nginx's IP — investigate cvar / IoC registration / wrapper UDS connectivity.

### Step 10 — Sustained monitoring

Watch for 30 minutes:

- `tail -f /tmp/ss14-wrapper.log` on pico (no unexpected errors)
- `tail -f /tmp/Library/Logs/ss14-watchdog/...` (Robust.Server logs)
- nginx error log on zig-computer: `sudo tail -f /var/log/nginx/error.log`
- Real player connections (organic traffic — wait for natural population)

Any sustained errors (per-minute disconnects, NaN rates, latency spikes) → roll back.

**Rollback**: full revert to Steps 7 → 5 sequence (re-enable zig watchdog, remove nginx stream block).

### Step 11 — Documentation

After 30+ minutes of stability:

- Flip dotfiles-9g1 spec bead → closed (work is shipped)
- Update zig-zone runbook: mark Phase 4 as SHIPPED-via-C2 (was originally rolled back; the eventual win was a different architecture than originally attempted)
- Drop a `note` bead with the cutover-date + observed metrics (latency p50/p99, ban_address real-IP confirmed, etc.)
- Brief Discord / community channels if applicable

**Rollback**: N/A (post-stability documentation).

## Total rollback (nuclear)

If anything goes catastrophically wrong at any step:

```bash
# On zig-computer
sudo rm /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-game.conf
sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx
sudo systemctl enable --now ss14-watchdog ss14-replay-rotate.timer

# On pico
ssh pico 'launchctl unload ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ss14-watchdog.plist'
ssh pico 'launchctl unload ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ss14-replay-rotate.plist'
ssh pico 'cp /tmp/vs14-config.bak.* /opt/ss14-watchdog/instances/vacation-station/config.toml'
ssh pico 'launchctl kickstart -k gui/$(id -u pico)/com.zig.ss14-wrapper'   # reset upstream port
```

SS14 back on zig in <60s. Wrapper still running on pico but idle (no upstream traffic).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Pre-flight checklist all green
- [ ] Steps 1-3 complete; wrapper + Robust.Server healthy on pico
- [ ] Step 4 synthetic test PASSES (real IP arrives in ENUMERATE)
- [ ] Steps 5-7 complete; zig-computer ss14-watchdog stopped + disabled
- [ ] Step 8 end-to-end client test PASSES
- [ ] Step 9 ban_address shows real IP (not 100.72.47.4)
- [ ] Step 10 30-min monitoring clean (no sustained errors)
- [ ] Step 11 documentation updates pushed
- [ ] zig-zone runbook Phase 4 flipped to SHIPPED-via-C2
- [ ] dotfiles-9g1 spec bead closed
- [ ] Cutover-receipt note bead created with metrics
notes:
## CUTOVER PAUSED 2026-05-24 — wrapper-impl bug surfaced live

Steps 1-7 completed cleanly:
- pico vs14 pulled + submodule synced to azigler/RobustToolbox @ 85982545e
- Content.Server published with FullRelease + osx-arm64 + self-contained → /tmp/ss14-publish → rsync'd to /Users/pico/ss14-watchdog/instances/vacation-station/bin/
- config.toml: added [net] use_wrapper_remote_address=true + wrapper_socket=/tmp/ss14-wrapper.sock + bindto=127.0.0.1 + port=1213; [status] bind=*:1213
- watchdog appsettings ApiPort flipped 1212 → 1213
- pico watchdog loaded, Robust.Server running on 127.0.0.1:1213 (UDP) + *:1213 (TCP)
- wrapper UPSTREAM flipped 11215 → 1213 (with launchctl unload+load — kickstart -k doesn't re-read plist env vars — runbook gotcha worth)
- Synthetic UDP from zig → wrapper → ENUMERATE shows real IP 192.0.2.99 ✓
- zig watchdog stopped + disabled + Robust.Server killed
- nginx stream block at /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-game.conf added with proxy_protocol on
- nginx -t + reload OK; :1212 bound by nginx workers
- /status + /info via the pipe returned valid JSON (after flipping [status] bind to wildcard *:1213 to fix Host-header mismatch — Robust HttpListener prefix-based routing was rejecting Host: ss14.zig.computer with 127.0.0.1:1213 prefix)

Step 8 (real client connection) FAILED with handshake timeout. Root cause = wrapper-impl bug (see dotfiles-jc5):
- nginx UDP stream PROXY-protocol emits header ONCE per src-tuple session
- Wrapper code re-parses every datagram, drops if no PROXY signature
- First Lidgren handshake packet parses → session created. Subsequent packets drop → handshake stalls → timeout.

The /check OQ-03 finding ("PROXY on every datagram") was wrong — it generalized from a synthetic test that used different src ports per packet (so each was its own session, each got a header).

ROLLED BACK cleanly:
- nginx stream block removed + reload
- zig ss14-watchdog re-enabled + started
- SS14 back on zig (round 19, 0 players, /status OK)
- pico wrapper still running (idle, harmless — no traffic routed to it)
- pico Robust.Server still running (will idle out / be cleaned up by next session)
- config.toml + watchdog appsettings on pico STILL HAVE the cutover edits (not rolled back; next session can re-apply on top of the wrapper fix)

To resume: dispatch /fix on dotfiles-jc5 (full TDD wave — wrapper LookupBySrcTuple + integration test), /scrutinize, re-cutover with new wrapper binary.

title: zig-zone Phase 2: migrate Ollama + Phoenix to pico
description:
## Context
Phase 2 of zig-zone (see `~/explore/.claude/skills/zig-zone/SKILL.md`). Phase 1 SHIPped (mesh live + ACL + Tailscale SSH bidirectional). Now move Ollama + Phoenix off zig-computer onto pico (the Mac Studio M1 Max 64GB), reached via tailnet.

## Discovered state at start
- **pico**: Ollama running via `brew services`, but bound to STALE tailnet IP `100.95.4.73` (current IP is `100.72.47.4`). Pico's IP drifted after rename/rebind in Phase 1; OLLAMA_HOST was set with the old IP. Curl from zig-computer to current IP/hostname fails — port unreachable until rebind.
- **pico**: `uv` installed at `/Users/pico/.local/bin/uv`; Python 3.9.6 system. No Phoenix yet.
- **zig-computer**: `ollama.service` (system-level): active, listening `127.0.0.1:11434`. PID 1339.
- **zig-computer**: `lb-phoenix.service` (user-level): active, listening `127.0.0.1:6006` (UI) + `*:4317` (gRPC OTLP). Working dir `/home/ubuntu/linearb/agent-factory/factory/o11y/phoenix`. Data dir 767MB (sqlite + WAL + inferences + trace_datasets).

## Task
1. Fix pico's OLLAMA_HOST → current tailnet IP `100.72.47.4`, restart, verify binding
2. Pull models on pico: `llama3.2:3b`, `qwen2.5:7b`
3. Install Phoenix on pico via uv tool
4. Stop Phoenix on zig-computer; scp data dir to pico via tailnet
5. Create LaunchAgent on pico bound to tailnet IP for Phoenix
6. Start Phoenix on pico; verify UI + OTLP endpoints reachable from zig-computer via tailnet
7. Stop + disable `ollama.service` + `lb-phoenix.service` on zig-computer
8. Verify localhost endpoints refuse on zig-computer
9. Update any OTEL env vars / agentic loop configs (factory etc.) to point at pico tailnet IP for traces
10. Document in runbook: bind-on-startup pattern, restart sequence, migration commands

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `curl http://pico:11434/api/tags` from zig-computer returns 200 w/ model list
- [ ] `curl http://pico:6006/` from zig-computer returns Phoenix UI
- [ ] `curl http://pico:4317` from zig-computer accepts OTLP traces (HTTP 200 / gRPC ack)
- [ ] zig-computer `127.0.0.1:11434` refuses (ollama.service stopped + disabled)
- [ ] zig-computer `127.0.0.1:6006` refuses (lb-phoenix.service stopped + disabled)
- [ ] Phoenix data migrated — historic traces still visible at `http://pico:6006/`
- [ ] OTEL configs (factory + any other consumers) point at pico
- [ ] Runbook gotcha added for the bind-on-startup / IP drift pattern
acceptance_criteria:
- [x] curl http://pico:11434/api/tags from zig-computer returns 200 w/ model list (llama3.2:3b + qwen2.5:7b)
 - [x] curl http://pico:6006/ from zig-computer returns Phoenix UI (200)
 - [x] curl http://pico:6006/v1/traces from zig-computer accepts OTLP HTTP (200)
 - [x] OTLP gRPC at pico:4317 listening (*:4317 from lsof)
 - [x] zig-computer 127.0.0.1:11434 refuses (ollama.service stopped + disabled)
 - [x] zig-computer 127.0.0.1:6006 refuses (lb-phoenix.service stopped + disabled)
 - [x] Phoenix data migrated — 767MB SQLite tarred (128MB compressed), streamed via Tailscale SSH, untarred on pico
 - [x] OTEL configs updated — 6 agent .env.local files (accounts, fastlane, fleet, gtm, listener, product) now PHOENIX_COLLECTOR_URL=http://pico:6006
 - [x] Runbook gotchas added — #15 (brew services regenerates plist), #16 (system Python too old for Phoenix), #17 (scp host-key fix via tailscale ssh pipe)
